records.Statutes: False Claims Act (FCA, 31 U.S.C. 3729); Anti-Kickback Statute (AKS, 42 U.S.C. 1320a-7b)
records.Summary:
- Government alleged Novartis ran tens of thousands of sham speaker programs from 2002 to 2011 that were really just bribes to doctors
- Paid honoraria for talks that were barely given or never happened at all, sometimes with fake restaurant receipts and gift cards to high prescribers
- Held events at top-end restaurants (Masa, Le Bernardin, Peter Luger), plus fishing trips, wineries, golf clubs, even Hooters, way over the $125 per person meal cap
- Sales reps picked high-volume prescribers as paid speakers to get them writing more, and dropped them if scripts didn't go up
- Drugs were cardiovascular and diabetes meds reimbursed by Medicare, Medicaid, TRICARE, and VA
- Novartis admitted to specific conduct and the scheme traced back to top management at the NJ headquarters
records.Notes:
- Rare case where the company made factual admissions, not just a no-admit settlement
- 5 year CIA forced speaker programs to virtual-only and slashed the spend
- Relator was a former sales rep (Oswald Bilotta), filed 2011, qui tam
- Money split: $591.4M federal FCA, $38.4M AKS forfeiture, $48.2M states

records.Statutes: Food, Drug & Cosmetic Act ; False Claims Act
records.Summary:
- Warner-Lambert's Parke-Davis unit promoted Neurontin (gabapentin) for off-label uses it was never approved for: pain, bipolar, migraine, ADHD
- Used CME events, paid physician speakers, and ghostwritten or biased journal articles to push the unapproved uses
- Made false or misleading claims to providers about how well it worked and whether FDA had approved those uses
- Drug was only FDA-approved for epilepsy seizures at the time
- Pleaded guilty to misbranding and introducing an unapproved new drug into interstate commerce
records.Notes:
- Settling entity was Warner-Lambert (Parke-Davis division); Pfizer inherited liability after acquiring W-L in 2000
- Relator David Franklin (former medical liaison) got over $26M
- $240M criminal fine, rest civil; second-largest healthcare fraud penalty at the time
- Same drug generated a later $325M payer settlement in 2014, so one product can mean multiple records
- Confirm exact federal/state split against the primary DOJ source before finalizing

records.Summary:
- Warner Chilcott's U.S. sales unit pleaded guilty to felony health care fraud over the illegal marketing of seven drugs, including Actonel, Asacol, Atelvia, and Loestrin
- It paid kickbacks to doctors through "Medical Education Events," dinners and receptions at expensive restaurants with little or no educational content, and through speaker programs where the speakers often didn't discuss anything clinical
- Speakers who weren't prescribing enough were told they wouldn't be paid for future events unless their prescribing went up
- Sales reps also submitted false prior authorization requests for Atelvia and Actonel, using canned justifications and sometimes posing as physicians, to get past insurer rules that favored cheaper drugs
- Management had reps push an unsupported superiority claim for Actonel, even using a water-and-syrup sponge prop to fake a "mechanism of action" advantage
records.Notes:
- Mixed resolution: a felony guilty plea with a $22.94M criminal fine, plus a $102.06M civil settlement ($91.5M federal, $10.6M state) - The guilty plea carried admissions; the civil claims were allegations only
- Several individuals were also charged, including former president W. Carl Reichel (kickback conspiracy), two district managers, and a prescribing physician - Relator case was U.S. ex rel. Alexander; the whistleblowers received about $22.9M from the federal share - Conduct ran 2009 to 2013 (kickbacks) and 2011 to 2013 (prior authorizations); D. Mass.

records.Statutes: False Claims Act ; Anti-Kickback Statute;Civil forfeiture statute
records.Summary:
- Government alleged Novartis paid kickbacks to specialty pharmacies (BioScrip, Accredo, US Bioservices) to push two of its drugs, Exjade and Myfortic
- For Exjade, Novartis set up a closed network of three pharmacies and leaned on them to have nurses call patients and talk up refills while downplaying serious risks like kidney and liver failure
- It handed out more patient referrals and higher rebates to the pharmacies that hit higher refill rates, a scheme that ran 2008 to 2012
- For Myfortic, it offered rebates to five pharmacies in exchange for recommending that doctors switch patients off competitor drugs
- The drugs were reimbursed by Medicare and Medicaid, and Novartis made extensive factual admissions about its pharmacy relationships
records.Notes:
- Third settlement in the same lawsuit; BioScrip and Accredo paid $75M earlier, bringing the total recovery to $465M
- Of the $370M, $286.87M went to the federal government and $83.13M to states, plus a separate $20M forfeiture
- Novartis amended its existing CIA and extended it five years, adding independent review of pharmacy relationships
- Relator David Kester, qui tam; SDNY, approved by Judge Colleen McMahon
- Different Novartis matter from the $678M speaker-program case in row 1

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Daiichi paid physicians kickbacks as speaker fees through its POD programs (Physician Organization and Discussion) and other speaker programs
- Payments went out even when physicians took turns speaking on the same topics over Daiichi-paid dinners
- In some cases the speaker addressed only their own office staff, or the dinner blew past Daiichi's own $140 per person cap
- The drugs were Azor, Benicar, Tribenzor, and Welchol, all reimbursed by federal health programs
- The fees were meant to induce prescriptions, so the resulting claims were false
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only with no determination of liability
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG requiring internal compliance reforms
- Relator Kathy Fragoules, a former Daiichi sales rep, qui tam; received $6.1M
- POD programs ran Jan 2005 to Mar 2011; other speaker programs Jan 2004 to Feb 2011

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Salix used its speaker programs as a vehicle to pay kickbacks to doctors to prescribe its products
- Many programs were really social dinners at high-end restaurants like Nobu and Le Bernardin where the speaker barely discussed the drug, or not at all
- Salix ran about 10,000 speaker programs and kept inviting the same high-prescribing doctors to the same talk on the same topic
- For pre-recorded doc-in-the-box programs, the video often was not played, yet the speaker still got paid
- Covered Products included Xifaxan, Apriso, Relistor, MoviPrep, OsmoPrep, Solesta, and Deflux, all reimbursed by federal programs
records.Notes:
- Salix admitted, acknowledged, and accepted responsibility for the conduct
- $46.53M went to the federal government and $7.47M to states
- Salix spent about $25M on speaker payments and meals; honoraria ran $250 to $4,500, with several doctors earning over $100,000
- Covered Period was Jan 2009 through Dec 2013; SDNY, approved by Judge Denise Cote

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Forest paid kickbacks to doctors through speaker programs for Bystolic, Savella, and Namenda
- The payments and meals went out even when programs were cancelled, when no licensed providers showed up, or when the same people attended repeatedly
- Some meals also blew past Forest's own internal cost limits
- The drugs were reimbursed by federal health programs, so the inducements caused false claims
- Conduct ran January 2008 through December 2011
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only
- $35.5M went to the federal government and $2.5M to state Medicaid programs
- Relator Kurt Kroening, a former Forest employee, qui tam; received about $7.8M
- Filed in Milwaukee, Eastern District of Wisconsin

records.Statutes: False Claims Act ; Anti-Kickback Statute; Health Care Fraud
records.Summary:
- Aegerion resolved criminal and civil charges over its marketing of Juxtapid, approved only for a rare genetic cholesterol disorder (HoFH)
- It pleaded guilty to an FDCA violation for promoting Juxtapid as a general high-cholesterol drug and for failing to follow the required safety (REMS) program
- It also entered a deferred prosecution agreement admitting it conspired to grab patients' health information without authorization for commercial use
- On the civil side it made false statements to doctors, altered statements of medical necessity, and covered patient copays through a foundation in violation of the Anti-Kickback Statute
- The drug was reimbursed by federal programs, so the conduct caused false claims
records.Notes:
- Mixed resolution: a guilty plea with a $7.2M criminal fine and forfeiture, a HIPAA deferred prosecution agreement, and a $28.8M civil FCA settlement
- Also entered an FDA consent decree and a five-year CIA with HHS-OIG
- Relators Michele Clarke, Tricia Mullins, and Kristi Winger Szudlo, qui tam; received $4.7M total
- Conduct ran December 2012 to December 2015; D. Mass.
- Spans several categories at once: off-label, false statements, kickbacks, and a HIPAA privacy charge

records.Summary:
- Government alleged United Therapeutics used a charitable foundation as a conduit to pay the Medicare copays of patients taking its pulmonary arterial hypertension drugs
- The drugs were Adcirca, Remodulin, Tyvaso, and Orenitram
- UT pulled data from the foundation on how many of its patients were being helped and how much was spent, then used that to size its donations
- At the same time it blocked Medicare patients from its free drug program, steering them to the foundation so their purchases would bill Medicare
- Paying those copays to induce purchases violated the Anti-Kickback Statute and caused false claims
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG requiring independent review of patient-assistance arrangements
- Covered conduct ran February 2010 through January 2014; D. Mass.
- One of the wave of copay-foundation cases brought out of the District of Massachusetts

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Pfizer used a charitable foundation as a conduit to cover the Medicare copays of patients taking three of its drugs
- For Sutent and Inlyta, it moved patients out of its free drug program and over to the foundation so their purchases would generate Medicare revenue
- For Tikosyn, Pfizer raised the price 44 percent in late 2015, then worked with the foundation to open and fund a copay fund timed to that increase
- Tikosyn patients ended up being nearly all of that fund's beneficiaries
- Covering those copays to induce purchases violated the Anti-Kickback Statute and caused false claims
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; the U.S. Attorney noted Pfizer stepped forward to resolve it
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG covering patient-assistance arrangements
- Drugs were Sutent and Inlyta (renal cell carcinoma) and Tikosyn (arrhythmia); D. Mass.
- This is Pfizer's copay case, separate from its older Neurontin off-label matter

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Actelion used a charitable foundation as a conduit to pay the Medicare copays of patients taking its pulmonary arterial hypertension drugs
- The drugs were Tracleer, Ventavis, Veletri, and Opsumit
- Actelion pulled detailed data from the foundation and set its donations so the money would cover its own patients' copays but not patients on competitors' drugs
- It also blocked Medicare patients from its free drug program and steered them to the foundation instead
- While doing this it raised Tracleer's price at nearly 30 times the rate of inflation, and the copay payments violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only
- Johnson & Johnson acquired Actelion in June 2017, after the conduct; J&J was not involved
- Covered conduct was in 2014 and 2015; D. Mass.
- Part of the copay-foundation wave alongside United Therapeutics and Gilead's Letairis case

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Jazz used the Caring Voice Coalition foundation as a conduit to cover Medicare copays for its drugs Xyrem and Prialt
- In 2011 Jazz asked the foundation to create a narcolepsy fund that, in practice, almost only helped Xyrem patients, with Jazz as the sole donor
- It set up a similar severe chronic pain fund that almost only helped Prialt patients, and the foundation kept that fund off its website so Jazz was the main source of referrals
- Jazz also changed its free drug program so Medicare patients no longer qualified, steering them to the foundation instead
- While doing this it raised Xyrem's price sharply, and the copay payments violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; $28.5M of the $57M was restitution
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG covering patient-assistance arrangements
- Drugs were Xyrem (narcolepsy) and Prialt (severe chronic pain); D. Mass.
- Announced together with Lundbeck and Alexion as a combined $122.6M copay-foundation action
- Jazz raised Xyrem's list price about 150 percent during the covered period

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Lundbeck used a charitable foundation as a conduit to cover Medicare and ChampVA copays for its drug Xenazine
- Lundbeck donated to a fund that was supposed to help Huntington's Disease patients but in practice just covered Xenazine copays no matter what condition the drug was treating
- After regulators warned about this in 2014, Lundbeck and the foundation agreed to keep paying those copays out of a general fund instead
- When Lundbeck asked if regulators might object, the foundation replied that they did not know what the general fund was used for
- The conduct ran through 2016, Xenazine's price rose sharply, and the copay payments violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG covering patient-assistance arrangements
- Drug was Xenazine, for chorea in Huntington's Disease; D. Mass.
- Announced together with Jazz and Alexion as a combined $122.6M copay-foundation action
- Lundbeck raised Xenazine's price at over 22 times the rate of inflation

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Alexion used a charitable foundation as a conduit to cover Medicare copays for its drug Soliris, which can cost over $500,000 a year
- Alexion asked the foundation to create a fund and made clear it wanted the money to support only Soliris patients, becoming the fund's sole donor
- It tracked whether patients had stopped taking Soliris so its donations would not go to anyone off the drug
- It also kept Medicare patients out of its free drug program and steered them to the foundation instead
- Paying those copays to induce Soliris purchases violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; $7.02M of the $13M was restitution
- No CIA required; OIG cited Alexion's sweeping changes, including a new leadership team, half a new board, and a relocated headquarters
- Drug was Soliris (PNH and aHUS); covered period Jan 2010 to June 2016; D. Mass.
- Announced together with Jazz and Lundbeck as a combined $122.6M copay-foundation action

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Astellas used two charitable foundations as conduits to cover Medicare copays for its prostate cancer drug Xtandi
- Xtandi is an androgen receptor inhibitor, so Astellas asked the foundations to open funds covering only that drug class, where its main competitors were generic or low-share
- Astellas was the only donor to both funds, and Xtandi patients received nearly all of the assistance
- It even promoted the existence of these funds to persuade doctors to prescribe Xtandi over competing drugs
- Paying those copays to induce purchases violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; $50M of the $100M was restitution
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG covering patient-assistance arrangements
- Drug was Xtandi for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer; covered period Jul 2013 to Dec 2014; D. Mass.
- Foundations were Patient Access Network Foundation and Chronic Disease Fund

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Amgen used charitable foundations as conduits to cover Medicare copays for its drugs Sensipar and Kyprolis
- For Sensipar, Amgen got a foundation to open a fund covering only that drug and was its sole donor, even though it knew giving the drug away free would have cost less than funding the copays
- For Kyprolis, which Onyx had marketed before Amgen acquired it, the company funded a travel fund and copay fund that almost only helped Kyprolis patients
- The drugs were reimbursed by Medicare, so steering patients to these funds generated revenue and caused false claims
- Paying those copays to induce purchases violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; $12.375M of the $24.75M was restitution
- Entered a CIA with HHS-OIG as part of the settlement
- Drugs were Sensipar (secondary hyperparathyroidism) and Kyprolis (multiple myeloma); D. Mass.
- Kyprolis conduct traces to Onyx, which Amgen acquired in 2013
- Foundations were Patient Access Network Foundation and Chronic Disease Fund

records.Summary:
- Government resolved criminal and civil cases over Insys's marketing of Subsys, a powerful sublingual fentanyl spray
- Insys used sham speaker programs to pay bribes and kickbacks to doctors and nurse practitioners for writing more Subsys prescriptions and at higher doses
- The kickbacks also took the form of jobs for prescribers' relatives and friends and lavish meals and entertainment
- Insys pushed Subsys for non-cancer patients it was not meant for and lied to insurers about diagnoses to get claims paid
- One physician assistant wrote no Subsys scripts until joining the speaker program, then wrote about 672 and took $44,000 in kickbacks
records.Notes:
- Mixed resolution: $195M civil FCA settlement plus $30M criminal ($2M fine and $28M forfeiture); a subsidiary pleaded guilty to five counts of mail fraud
- Insys admitted to a detailed statement of facts; eight company executives were separately convicted
- Entered a five-year deferred prosecution agreement and an unprecedented five-year CIA with OIG
- Conduct ran August 2012 to June 2015; handled in D. Mass. and C.D. Cal.
- Resolved five qui tam suits; the whistleblowers' share was not yet set at announcement

records.Summary:
- Government charged Avanir over kickbacks tied to its drug Nuedexta, approved only for a condition called pseudobulbar affect (PBA)
- Avanir paid a doctor to become a high prescriber and used speaker programs that were primarily social events with no real educational value
- It also pushed Nuedexta off-label in long-term care facilities, coaching its sales force to claim dementia patients had PBA-like behaviors to drive use
- One paid-speaker doctor had entire units of patients on Nuedexta at his facility
- The drug was reimbursed by federal programs, so the kickbacks and off-label marketing caused false claims
records.Notes:
- Mixed resolution: a deferred prosecution agreement with a $7.8M penalty and about $5.07M forfeiture, plus about $96M civil and $7M to states
- Four individuals were separately indicted, including former Avanir employees and a top prescriber
- Entered a CIA with HHS-OIG
- Relators Kevin Manieri (about $12.39M) and Duane Arnold and Mark Shipman (about $5.37M total), qui tam
- Handled in the Northern District of Ohio and Northern District of Georgia

records.Summary: - This is the complaint stage of the matter resolved in row 26. The United States filed its complaint over Teva's Copaxone copay scheme in the District of Massachusetts in August 2020, and Teva settled it, along with a related generic price-fixing matter, for $450 million in October 2024
records.Notes:
- Same $450M matter as row 26, which is the final settlement
- Conduct ran 2006 to 2017; the copay complaint was filed in D. Mass in August 2020

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Gilead used a charitable foundation as a conduit to cover the Medicare copays of patients taking its pulmonary arterial hypertension drug Letairis
- From 2007 through 2010, Gilead paid the foundation, which then covered Letairis patients' copays
- Gilead pulled data from the foundation on its spending and used it to size donations that would cover only its own patients
- It also steered Medicare patients to the foundation so their purchases would bill Medicare
- During this period Gilead raised Letairis's price at over seven times the rate of inflation, and the copay payments violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG
- Drug was Letairis, for pulmonary arterial hypertension; conduct 2007 to 2010; D. Mass.
- Same PAH copay-foundation pattern as Actelion and United Therapeutics, all using Caring Voice Coalition
- This is Gilead's Letairis matter, separate from its later $202M HIV speaker-program case in row 27

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Biogen, with help from a specialty pharmacy, used two foundations as conduits to cover Medicare copays for its MS drugs Avonex and Tysabri
- Biogen identified Medicare-eligible patients in its own free drug program and moved them to the foundations so their purchases would bill Medicare instead
- It timed its donations and routed batches of copay applications through the specialty pharmacy, Advanced Care Scripts
- The foundations then covered those patients' copays, generating Medicare revenue for Biogen
- Using the foundations this way to induce purchases violated the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; the specialty pharmacy, Advanced Care Scripts, separately paid $1.4M
- Drugs were Avonex and Tysabri, both for multiple sclerosis; conduct around 2011 to 2013; D. Mass.
- Relator received about $3.96M; qui tam
- Foundations were Chronic Disease Fund and The Assistance Fund, which had separately paid $2M and $4M in 2019
- This is Biogen's $22M copay case, separate from its later $900M speaker-program case in row 23

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Incyte used an independent foundation as a conduit to pay the copays of patients taking its drug Jakafi
- Incyte was the sole donor to a fund opened to assist only myelofibrosis patients
- It then used that fund to cover copays for Jakafi patients who did not actually have myelofibrosis and were not eligible
- Incyte managers pressured the foundation by phone and email to help those ineligible patients, and its contractor helped them complete applications
- The drug was reimbursed by Medicare and TRICARE, so the conduct caused false claims
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement
- Drug was Jakafi, approved to treat myelofibrosis; conduct Nov 2011 to Dec 2014; E.D. Pa.
- Relator Justin Dillon, a former compliance executive at Incyte, qui tam; received about $3.59M
- Part of the broader copay-foundation enforcement wave

records.Summary:
- Government resolved a whistleblower's claims that Biogen paid kickbacks to doctors to prescribe its MS drugs Avonex, Tysabri, and Tecfidera
- Biogen offered speaker honoraria, speaker training fees, consulting fees, and meals to providers who spoke at or attended its programs
- The payments were meant to induce those providers to write more prescriptions
- The drugs were reimbursed by Medicare and Medicaid, so the kickbacks caused false claims
- The relator pursued the case himself for over seven years without the government intervening
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; $843.8M went to the federal government and $56.2M to 15 states
- Relator Michael Bawduniak, a former Biogen employee, litigated it without US intervention and received about 29.6 percent of the federal proceeds, roughly $250M
- Drugs were Avonex, Tysabri, and Tecfidera; conduct Jan 2009 to March 2014; D. Mass.
- This is Biogen's $900M speaker-program case, separate from its earlier $22M copay matter in row 21
- One of the largest speaker-program settlements on record

records.Statutes: False Claims Act ; Anti-Kickback Statute ; Medicaid Rebate Statute
records.Summary:
- Government resolved two schemes by Mallinckrodt tied to its drug Acthar Gel for a combined $260 million
- First, it underpaid Medicaid rebates by treating Acthar as a new drug first sold in 2013, which let it ignore years of earlier price increases even though the drug had been approved since 1952
- By 2013 Acthar's price had already climbed to over $28,000 per vial, so ignoring those increases sharply cut its rebate payments
- Second, it used a foundation as a conduit to pay illegal copay subsidies for Acthar so it could market the drug as free while raising the price
- Mallinckrodt admitted Acthar was not a new drug and agreed to correct the pricing baseline
records.Notes:
- Of the $260M, about $234.7M resolved the Medicaid rebate claims and about $26.3M resolved the kickback claims
- Mallinckrodt admitted the rebate facts; the kickback claims were allegations only
- Five-year CIA with HHS-OIG, including drug-price transparency provisions; settlement approved in bankruptcy by the District of Delaware
- Relators Strunck (about $4.9M, kickback) and Landolt (about $24.7M, rebate), qui tam
- Rebate matter filed in D. Mass and kickback matter in E.D. Pa

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Ultragenyx paid kickbacks to drive prescriptions of its drug Crysvita, which treats a rare bone disorder called XLH
- Ultragenyx paid a lab to run free genetic tests for patients and then paid again to get the results back, including which provider ordered each test
- Its sales force used those results to target providers whose patients tested positive and push them to prescribe Crysvita
- Because an insurer or doctor often needed a positive genetic test before Crysvita would be covered or prescribed, the free tests worked as an inducement
- The drug was reimbursed by Medicare and Medicaid, so the conduct caused false claims
records.Notes:
- Ultragenyx admitted and accepted responsibility for the core facts
- This is a sponsored genetic-testing kickback, not a copay-foundation case
- Drug was Crysvita, for X-linked hypophosphatemia; conduct ran until April 2022; D. Mass.
- Relator Ruggiero, qui tam; received about $1.07M
- One of the first major enforcement actions over a sponsored genetic-testing program

records.Statutes: False Claims Act ; Anti-Kickback Statute; Sherman Act
records.Summary:
- Government resolved two kickback schemes by Teva for a combined $450 million
- First, Teva paid Medicare patients' copays for its multiple sclerosis drug Copaxone through two supposedly independent foundations and a specialty pharmacy while steadily raising the price
- Second, Teva conspired with other generic makers to fix prices on pravastatin and two other generic drugs, and the government treated those gains as illegal kickbacks
- Teva had already paid a separate $225 million criminal penalty to the Antitrust Division and admitted to the price-fixing conspiracy
- The settlement was based on Teva's ability to pay, and it is the largest of the copay-foundation settlements to date
records.Notes:
- Civil settlement with no admissions on the FCA side; the price-fixing piece had a separate criminal deferred prosecution agreement with admissions
- The $450M is on top of the $225M criminal antitrust penalty
- Copaxone copay conduct ran 2006 to 2017; copay matter in D. Mass, price-fixing matter in E.D. Pa
- Largest of the copay-foundation cases; the DOJ has now recovered over $1 billion across that wave
- Same matter tracked at the complaint stage in row 19

records.Summary:
- Government resolved claims that Gilead used its HIV speaker programs to funnel kickbacks to doctors who prescribed its HIV drugs
- It paid high-volume prescribers tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars in honoraria to serve as speakers, plus meals and travel
- Programs ran at high-end restaurants like the James Beard House, which alone hosted 157 of them, and the same doctors attended the same talk over and over for free lavish dinners
- It also paid for speakers to travel to desirable spots like Hawaii, Miami, and New Orleans, sometimes at the speaker's request
- One speaker took over $300,000 in honoraria and wrote scripts that drove over $6 million in federal claims
records.Notes:
- Civil settlement with extensive factual admissions by Gilead; $176.93M went to the federal government and the rest to states
- Drugs were Stribild, Genvoya, Complera, Odefsey, Descovy, and Biktarvy; conduct Jan 2011 to Nov 2017
- Approved by Judge Paul Engelmayer in the Southern District of New York, from a sealed qui tam suit
- Gilead spent over $20 million in speaking fees plus millions more on meals, alcohol, and travel

records.Summary:
- Government resolved claims that Biohaven, before Pfizer acquired it, paid kickbacks to doctors to prescribe its migraine drug Nurtec ODT
- Biohaven put certain providers on a Nurtec speaker bureau and paid them honoraria and meals at high-end restaurants to induce prescriptions
- Doctors who attended the same program repeatedly got no real educational benefit from the repeats
- Some programs were attended by people with no educational need to be there, like the speakers' spouses, family, friends, or office colleagues
- The drug was reimbursed by Medicare, so the kickbacks caused false claims; Pfizer ended the programs after acquiring Biohaven in October 2022
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; about $50.2M was the federal share and about $9.5M went to states
- Pfizer resolved this on behalf of its subsidiary Biohaven for conduct before the acquisition
- Drug was Nurtec ODT, for migraine; conduct March 2020 to September 2022; W.D.N.Y.
- Relator Patricia Frattasio, a former Biohaven sales rep, qui tam; received about $8.4M

records.Summary:
- Government resolved claims that Takeda paid kickbacks to doctors to prescribe its antidepressant Trintellix
- Takeda put certain providers on a Trintellix speaker bureau and paid them honoraria and meals at high-end restaurants to induce prescriptions
- Doctors who attended multiple programs on the same topic got no real educational benefit from the duplicates
- The drug was reimbursed by Medicare and other federal programs, so the conduct caused false claims
- Conduct ran from January 2014 to October 2020
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only
- Drug was Trintellix, an antidepressant for major depressive disorder; E.D. Cal.
- Resolved a qui tam matter; the release does not name the relator
- The DOJ tied the case to its new Task Force to Eliminate Fraud and National Fraud Enforcement Division

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Medisca, a supplier of ingredients to compounding pharmacies, reported false and inflated Average Wholesale Prices for two ingredients used in compounded drugs
- Federal programs base part of their compound reimbursement on those reported AWPs, so inflating them pumped up what the pharmacies got paid
- For resveratrol, Medisca bought it for about $0.37 a gram and sold it for under $2, but reported an AWP of $777 a gram, a spread of over $775 per gram
- For mometasone furoate, it bought the ingredient for under $8 a gram and sold it for over $1,000, but reported an AWP over $7,300 a gram, a spread of about $6,300 per gram
- Medisca allegedly dangled those big spreads to get compounding pharmacies to buy its ingredients, which let those pharmacies bill TRICARE and the Department of Labor program thousands of dollars per prescription
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only with no determination of liability
- This is an ingredient supplier and an AWP pricing scheme, so it sits a bit outside the manufacturer kickback and copay cases that fill most of this tracker
- Affected programs were the Defense Health Agency's TRICARE and the Department of Labor's Office of Workers' Compensation Programs
- Relator Doug McMakin, a pharmacist who ran a compounding pharmacy, qui tam; received about $3.43M
- Handled in the Eastern and Western Districts of Texas; DCIS and USPS-OIG investigated; archived DOJ release from November 2024

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Assertio, which was called Depomed at the time, caused false claims for its fentanyl nasal spray Lazanda for patients who did not have breakthrough cancer pain
- Lazanda is a TIRF drug approved only for breakthrough cancer pain in patients already on and tolerant to around-the-clock opioids
- From 2013 to 2017 Assertio aimed its marketing at pain specialists writing high volumes of TIRF products, including some who were flagged for diversion or later indicted
- It put those high-volume prescribers on its speakers' bureau and advisory boards, and built a "Signature Support Program" to get Lazanda scripts approved by insurers, including Medicare Part D
- The marketing led 13 high-volume prescribers to write Lazanda for Medicare and TRICARE patients without breakthrough cancer pain, so the resulting claims were false
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only with no determination of liability
- Assertio was formerly known as Depomed and is based in Lake Forest, Illinois
- Drug was Lazanda, a fentanyl nasal spray; conduct 2013 to 2017; D.D.C.
- Relators Noelle Webb and Nicole Novellino, both former Assertio sales reps, qui tam; received $657,000
- Same TIRF fentanyl and speaker-bureau pattern as the Insys Subsys case in record 17, though this one was civil only and much smaller

records.Statutes: Food, Drug & Cosmetic Act; Anti-Kickback Statute (AKS, 42 U.S.C. 1320a-7b)
records.Summary:
- Government said that from 2007 to 2017 Purdue illegally marketed its opioids to hundreds of prescribers it had good reason to believe were writing scripts with no legitimate medical purpose
- Purdue defrauded the DEA by misrepresenting how well its anti-diversion programs worked, then used scripts from those problem prescribers to justify requests to manufacture even more of its opioids
- To push doctors to prescribe more, Purdue paid kickbacks through its doctor speaker program and to an electronic health record platform
- The products were Purdue's prescription opioids, with OxyContin the best known
- Purdue pleaded guilty to one count of conspiracy to defraud the United States and violate the FDCA, plus two counts of conspiracy to violate the Anti-Kickback Statute
records.Notes:
- This is the April 2026 sentencing; Purdue had already pleaded guilty back in November 2020 to the three-count felony information
- The court ordered a $3.544B criminal fine, assessed through the bankruptcy, plus $2B in criminal forfeiture, so over $5B in criminal penalties total
- Up to $1.775B of the forfeiture gets credited if Purdue stops operating in its current form and emerges from bankruptcy as a public benefit company, with proceeds going to state and local opioid abatement
- Criminal case, not a qui tam; handled in the District of New Jersey with the District of Vermont; the FBI, HHS-OIG, and DEA investigated
- A separate civil resolution and the Sackler family payments are tracked elsewhere, so this row is just the criminal penalty

records.Statutes: False Claims Act (FCA, 31 U.S.C. 3729) - retained overpayments
records.Summary:
- Government alleged Oglethorpe and three of its top executives knowingly held onto Medicare overpayments that the company's own consultants had already flagged
- The overpayments came from admitting Medicare patients to two Ohio psychiatric hospitals, Ridgeview Behavioral Hospital and Georgetown Behavioral Hospital, and a substance abuse clinic, The Woods at Parkside
- Those patients did not actually qualify for inpatient psychiatric care, so the stays should not have been billed
- Keeping money you know you were overpaid is itself a False Claims Act violation, sometimes called a reverse false claim
- The conduct ran from 2021 to the present
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only
- Oglethorpe runs psychiatric hospitals out of Tampa, so this is a provider case, not a drug manufacturer case, and it falls outside the usual pharma pattern here
- The named executives are founder and owner Robert Cohen, CEO John Picciano, and COO James O'Shea
- Oglethorpe had signed a CIA with HHS-OIG in 2021 after an earlier FCA settlement, then violated it, so the defendants agreed to a 10-year exclusion from Medicare, Medicaid, and all federal programs starting July 2026
- Relators were four former employees (Whitney Treloar, Darren Caruso, Jeanette Skinner, Joel Snook), qui tam; their share was not yet set; M.D. Fla.

records.Summary:
- Government alleged Matrix and HealthFair caused Medicare Advantage plans to submit false or invalid diagnosis codes used for risk adjustment
- Medicare Advantage pays plans more for sicker patients, so tacking on unsupported diagnoses pulled in higher payments the plans were not entitled to
- Matrix, which does in-home health assessments, reported chronic conditions like diabetic retinopathy, atrial fibrillation, rheumatoid arthritis, and COPD that often were not supported and were not diagnosed by any other provider who saw the patient
- HealthFair, which ran mobile health buses, reported conditions like HIV/AIDS, metastatic cancer, and congestive heart failure with no documentation, based only on patient say-so, or even against test results
- The plans passed those diagnoses to CMS for inflated risk-adjusted payments, so the claims were false
records.Notes:
- No-admit civil settlement; claims were allegations only with no determination of liability
- This is a Medicare Advantage risk-adjustment case run by health-assessment companies, not a drug manufacturer case, so it sits outside the usual pharma pattern here
- Total $56.5M splits into $36.5M from Matrix, $5M from HealthFair, and $15M from HealthFair founder James Ekbatani
- Matrix conduct ran 2014 to 2019; HealthFair 2015 to 2017; Matrix acquired HealthFair in 2018 and shut it down by 2020
- Two separate qui tams: Nancy Cahill, a former Matrix employee, in S.D.N.Y. (about $7.3M), and Dr. Robert Oristaglio, HealthFair's former chief medical officer, in E.D. Tex. (about $3.6M)

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Overstated efficacy / unapproved uses / risk omission
records.SUMMARY:
- TV ad plus a podcast with their exec chairman called "Is the FDA BLOCKING Life Saving Cancer Treatments?"
- Claimed Anktiva can "cure" cancer and stop it from coming back, but it's only approved for a specific bladder cancer group (BCG-unresponsive NMIBC with CIS)
- The trial behind it was single-arm so you can't really claim durability or survival from it
- Podcast suggested it works in "all cancers" including lung cancer, which it's not approved for, and risk info was barely there
- Never submitted the podcast on Form 2253, and they'd already gotten untitled letters in Sept 2025 and Jan 2026 for similar claims and kept going
records.NOTES:
- Ignoring untitled letters escalates things fast. Podcasts count
- First OPDP warning letter of 2026 (posted 3/24/26)

records.SUMMARY:
- Instagram post called Addyi the "sex pill for women" and only talked benefits, zero risk info
- Never mentioned serious risks like low blood pressure and fainting
- Saying it's the "first FDA-approved treatment for women's sexual dysfunction" made it sound like it's for everyone, but it's only approved for premenopausal women with a specific condition (HSDD)
- Left out the limitations too: not for men, not for postmenopausal women, not for improving performance
- Also never submitted the post on Form 2253 (FDA posted the letter 6/10/2025)
records.NOTES:
- Social media gets the same fair balance rules as everything else
- Stretching the indication is its own violation

records.SUMMARY:
- Sales brochure for Breztri (COPD inhaler) claimed it improves all-cause mortality and cuts severe exacerbations
- The data they cited didn't actually back that up. The mortality claim was based on observational findings
- They added a small disclaimer saying the results were "observational in nature" but FDA said that doesn't fix a misleading headline and graph
- Big deal at the time since it was a normal sales aid for an approved drug, and the first OPDP warning letter since CytoDyn
records.NOTES:
- Claims have to match the data you cite
- A disclaimer in fine print doesn't save a misleading headline

records.SUMMARY:
- Then-CEO went on a video interview and promoted leronlimab as safe and effective for COVID even though it wasn't approved for anything
- Said "our results were really strong" when FDA said the data they submitted didn't show a strong connection
- Kept making up survival numbers ("78%... went up to 82%... maybe 88... 95"). FDA said none of that was true
- Also claimed the drug was "166% better" than placebo when the main trial didn't show meaningful data at all
- Pretty much said it was the only drug that helps critically ill COVID patients survive
- The repeated pattern of making up numbers plus promoting an unapproved use is what got them the letter
records.NOTES:
- Exec interviews count as promotion
- You can't call an investigational drug safe or effective, period

records.SUMMARY:
- Ran a sponsored video on a Boston NBC station's site called "Paragard: Family Planning During The Pandemic"
- It was basically a doctor interview hyping efficacy with no risk info at all
- Paragard's label has warnings for ectopic pregnancy, sepsis, PID, perforation, and expulsion. None of it got mentioned
- FDA said telling people to go check the website doesn't make up for leaving everything out
- They also never submitted the video on Form 2253, and they'd already gotten an untitled letter in 2019 for the same thing
records.NOTES:
- Getting an untitled letter and doing it again is how you end up with a warning letter
- Sponsored content on a news site still counts as promotion

records.SUMMARY:
- Marketed Dsuvia (a Schedule II opioid) with the tagline "Tongue and Done"
- The drug has a REMS. A provider has to place it under the tongue with a single-dose applicator and there's rules on redosing
- The tagline made it sound super simple and skipped all of that
- FDA said it was misleading on dosing and could actually cause medication errors with a strong opioid
records.NOTES: - Don't oversimplify how a high-risk drug gets administered, especially with a REMS

records.SUMMARY:
- Facebook post basically said Afrezza helps your body "work its best" with "no drama"
- Didn't include any risk info at all, and Afrezza literally has a boxed warning for bronchospasm in asthma/COPD patients
- FDA's issue was the post made it look like the drug has zero safety concerns
- They tried linking to the full PI and had a little hover popup with some risk info, but FDA said that doesn't fix anything
- Got flagged through the Bad Ad program
records.NOTES:
- Linking to the PI doesn't count as including risk info
- First OPDP warning letter over a social media post

records.SUMMARY:
- Two Sucraid HCP promotional emails ("unresolved IBS-like symptoms... Could it be CSID?") implied the drug treats IBS-like symptoms, creating a misleading impression it works for IBS when it's only approved for CSID
- Buried the full indication in small plain font at the bottom, after eye-catching graphics and colors, which FDA said wasn't enough to fix the misleading impression
- Omitted material risk info (severe hypersensitivity from papain, raised blood glucose in diabetics) and listed adverse reactions before the more serious precautions, minimizing the risks
records.NOTES: - Visual hierarchy is part of the message; a fine-print indication and out-of-order risk info can't cure a misleading impression created by headlines and graphics

records.SUMMARY:
- Brukinsa DTC TV ad "Clarity" used attention-grabbing visuals (a woman crossing a mountain bridge, "see your future clearly again") to suggest patients can set treatment concerns aside, overstating a quality-of-life benefit the open-label trial data doesn't support
- Claimed a superior safety profile with lower cardiovascular/afib risk versus Imbruvica, but the ALPINE trial wasn't powered to show a meaningful difference, so the comparative claim was unsupported
- Compelling visuals during the major statement interfered with comprehension of the risk information
records.NOTES: - Open-label PRO data and underpowered trials can't support quality-of-life or comparative safety claims, and dramatic visuals can't run over the major statement

records.SUMMARY:
- Nubeqa DTC YouTube video and a Spanish-language TV ad (patient "Harold" driving, walking by the ocean, eating at a carnival) made benefit claims for prostate cancer use but omitted material risk information
- Included a generic "serious side effects including heart disease and seizure" line but left out the specifics from the patient info: that the heart disease can lead to death and the seizure risk means avoiding activities where sudden loss of consciousness could cause serious harm
- Attention-grabbing visuals and frequent scene changes pulled attention away from the safety presentation
records.NOTES: - A generic side-effects line doesn't satisfy fair balance; ads must convey the actual severity (death, activity restrictions) and visuals can't drown out risk info

records.SUMMARY:
- Amvuttra consumer website (ATTR-CM page) ran the headline "Proven to help people with ATTR-CM live longer" with bold survival graphics
- The mortality figures (36% lower risk of death vs placebo over 3.5 years; 80% vs 72% survival at 3.5 years) came from the open-label extension of HELIOS-B, where everyone could take Amvuttra and there was no real placebo group
- OPDP said that extension was inadequately designed to support conclusory all-cause-mortality claims, so the certainty the page conveyed wasn't supported
records.NOTES: - Survival and mortality claims need a controlled comparison; numbers pulled from an open-label extension with no placebo arm overstate certainty

records.SUMMARY:
- Niktimvo branded website made efficacy claims for chronic GVHD such as "may offer fast and lasting responses" and "75% of people (59 of 79) experienced a response"
- The totality implied faster, complete, and more durable responses than AGAVE-201 showed, where the endpoint was an objective response (not complete response) and the median duration of response was only about 1.9 months
- Visual presentations also suggested organ-specific benefits beyond the evidence
records.NOTES: - Response-rate data can't be framed as fast, complete, or lasting when the median duration of response is short; net impression has to match the trial endpoints

records.SUMMARY:
- Adcetris Facebook video ads asked "Recently diagnosed with T-cell lymphoma?" and called it "an FDA-approved option for adults with certain CD30-expressing T-cell lymphomas"
- That overstates the approved indication, which is narrower (previously untreated sALCL or other CD30-expressing PTCL, in combination with CHP chemotherapy), implying broad use across T-cell lymphoma
- The full indication shown at the bottom of the ads wasn't enough to correct the misleading impression
records.NOTES: - Naming a broad disease when the approval is a specific subtype and combination overstates the indication, and fine-print labeling doesn't cure it

records.SUMMARY:
- Alymsys (a bevacizumab biosimilar) patient brochure omitted key indication details such as specific patient populations and the required use in combination with chemotherapy, implying it can be used broadly across cancers or as a single agent
- Failed to disclose serious risks, including that bevacizumab products can cause infusion-related reactions (hypertension, hypertensive crises, wheezing, oxygen desaturation, chest pain, rigors)
- Buried the limitations of use on page four, separated from the claims
records.NOTES: - A biosimilar's promo must carry the same indication limits and risk disclosures as the reference biologic; dropping combination and population context implies unsupported broad use

records.SUMMARY:
- Lucemyra web content overstated how much it helps with opioid withdrawal, framing it as a fuller detox or dependence fix
- Lucemyra only manages withdrawal symptoms during discontinuation, so the messaging implied a broader treatment benefit than the approved use
- Minimized serious risks such as hypotension, bradycardia, and QT prolongation
records.NOTES: - A symptom-management drug can't be framed as a cure or full treatment, and cardiovascular risks like hypotension and QT effects must be disclosed

records.SUMMARY:
- Ozempic DTC video ("Ozempic vs. - Random Guy") staged two characters, one for Ozempic and one for "other GLP-1s," with a narrator claiming Ozempic "is the GLP-1 with the most FDA-approved uses"
- That implies superiority over other GLP-1s for type 2 diabetes, but the supporting cardiovascular and kidney trials were placebo-controlled, not head-to-head, so superiority can't be determined
- Reinforced the impression with visual contrast (bright orange vs dull gray shirt) and a comedic dismissal of the competitor, and gave no signal that risk information was coming
records.NOTES: - "Most approved uses" plus visual and comedic contrast implies a superiority that placebo-controlled trials can't support; comparative claims need head-to-head data

records.SUMMARY:
- Tremfya DTC TV ad claimed "~1 out of 2 patients in clinical remission at 1 and 2 years" and "1 out of 3 achieved endoscopic remission at one year"
- Misleading because those rates come only from patients who first responded at 12 weeks; about 38-39% never responded and were excluded from the maintenance study, so the real long-term remission likelihood is lower
- Also excluded material risk information, including that Tremfya can lower the immune system's ability to fight infections
records.NOTES: - Remission rates measured only in initial responders overstate the odds for a new patient; denominators matter, and infection risk can't be dropped from the major statement

records.SUMMARY:
- Vonjo DTC TV ad ("turn the page with VONJO") used a pop-up-book theme showing an elderly man walking his dog, eating ice cream, and dancing, implying improved emotional and social functioning for myelofibrosis patients
- That overstates benefit: the PERSIST-2 symptom-score endpoint (>=50% TSS reduction) failed against best available therapy (p=0.08), so the quality-of-life impression isn't supported
- Also referenced "low" platelet counts without specifying the approved threshold (below 50 x 10^9/L), and the major statement ran during the distracting pop-up-book visuals
records.NOTES: - A failed symptom endpoint can't support quality-of-life claims; vague indication language and theme visuals over the major statement compound the problem

records.SUMMARY:
- Oral Wegovy (semaglutide tablets) DTC TV ad pushed an aspirational "live lighter" message and implied the pill form beats injectable GLP-1s
- Overstated expected weight-loss outcomes and conveyed unsupported impressions about efficacy and quality-of-life improvement
- Risk information wasn't presented with enough prominence relative to the benefit messaging
records.NOTES: - Dosage-form superiority claims ("the pill is better") need head-to-head evidence, and aspirational language can overstate weight-loss benefit

records.SUMMARY:
- Vyvgart Hytrulo DTC TV ad showed patients self-injecting "my way" while traveling and tailgating at a stadium ("can go where I go"), implying the drug is easy to administer anywhere
- That oversimplifies the approved Instructions for Use, which require ~30 minutes to reach room temperature on a clean flat surface, gathering supplies, hand washing, an abdominal injection, and sharps disposal
- Omitted hypersensitivity and injection-reaction risks (allergic reactions, raised blood pressure, chills, chest/stomach/back pain), and attention-grabbing visuals ran during the major statement
records.NOTES: - Convenience framing can't oversimplify a multi-step injection process, and a small "see Instructions for Use" line doesn't cure the misleading impression

records.SUMMARY:
- Second Vyvgart Hytrulo DTC TV ad, for CIDP, showed a patient golfing and driving ("live vividly," "hitting fairways with the fellas"), implying all CIDP patients return to a fully active life
- Paired with "proven to significantly reduce the risk of symptoms getting worse," it overstated the typical benefit beyond what the data support for every patient
- Cited for omitting or minimizing risk information and for unclear instructions for use, the same pattern as the companion letter
records.NOTES: - Reduced-worsening data isn't the same as restored function for all patients; aspirational depictions imply a universal benefit the trial doesn't support

records.SUMMARY:
- neffy (epinephrine nasal spray) DTC TV ad used "No needles! Goodbye!" and "Goodbye, needle fear" to imply patients can entirely avoid injectable epinephrine for allergic emergencies
- That's misleading: approved Patient Information directs patients to seek emergency care after neffy, which may still involve injectable epinephrine, and there's no evidence neffy resolves needle anxiety
- Also showed the device out of its blister pack in bags and a pocket, against labeling, a public-health concern since each single-use device could fail if damaged
records.NOTES: - A nasal alternative can't claim "no needles" when injectable backup may still be needed, and depicting off-label storage of a single-use rescue device is a safety problem

records.SUMMARY:
- Anktiva HCP and consumer website pages claimed "84% of responders cystectomy-free at 36 months" and "99% disease-specific overall survival at 36 months" for BCG-unresponsive bladder cancer
- These overstate efficacy because the approval study QUILT-3.032 was single-arm with no comparator, so time-to-event endpoints like cystectomy avoidance and survival can't be attributed to Anktiva versus the natural disease course
- Footnote disclaimers noting the single-arm limitations didn't cure the misleading impression
records.NOTES: - Single-arm trials can't support time-to-event efficacy claims like survival or cystectomy avoidance, and disclaimers don't fix a headline number the design can't establish

records.SUMMARY:
- Brukinsa 60-second DTC TV ad showed a CLL patient hiking and ascending a mountain ("the CLL treatment that keeps you in control"), implying patients can travel long distances and keep an active lifestyle
- That impression is misleading because adverse reactions are most likely early in treatment or at dose changes, and patients need regular HCP monitoring
- The major statement wasn't presented in dual modality (audio and on-screen text together), and attention-grabbing visuals like embracing family ran during it
records.NOTES: - Lifestyle imagery can imply control that downplays early adverse-reaction risk and monitoring needs; the major statement must use concurrent dual modality

records.SUMMARY:
- Nexlizet sales aid hyped the lipid-lowering and cardiovascular benefit
- Played down the role of statins and overstated the drug's standalone benefit beyond the approved use and the evidence
- Glossed over the required risk information, leaving the claims unbalanced
records.NOTES: - Add-on lipid therapy can't be framed as a statin substitute, and benefit claims need balanced risk disclosure

records.SUMMARY:
- Tevimbra promotion used branded Microsoft Teams meeting backgrounds to make benefit claims for the PD-1 inhibitor
- Made anti-cancer benefit claims without the required risk information
- Didn't clarify the drug's specific approved indications, creating a misleading impression of broad use
records.NOTES: - Even novel formats like branded video-call backgrounds are promotional; benefit claims there still need risk info and accurate indication context

records.SUMMARY:
- Cobenfy DTC TV ad ("Bryan's Story") claimed "overall improvement across a range of schizophrenia symptoms" with overlapping "positive" and "negative symptom" circles, implying it improves negative symptoms as a distinct group
- The pivotal placebo-controlled trials measured overall severity (PANSS total), not positive vs negative domains, and a voiceover ("if you still have symptoms...") implied a treatment-refractory benefit that wasn't studied
- Also misclassified the drug as "not an antipsychotic," and attention-grabbing visuals (skateboarding, DJing) ran during the major statement
records.NOTES: - Claims have to match what the trial measured; you can't split out negative symptoms, imply treatment-refractory benefit, or relabel an antipsychotic by its mechanism

records.SUMMARY:
- Turalio patient video used dramatized before-and-after scenes implying everyone with tenosynovial giant cell tumor (TGCT) fully recovers
- The real response numbers are lower, so the depiction overstated typical outcomes
- Didn't adequately convey serious risks, including the boxed warning for hepatotoxicity
records.NOTES: - Dramatized before-and-after recovery overstates typical response, and a boxed-warning liver risk can't be sidelined

records.SUMMARY:
- Xcopri piece covered the epilepsy (partial-onset seizure) efficacy but left out important safety information
- The benefit-forward presentation wasn't balanced against the drug's risk profile
- Skipped serious labeled warnings, such as DRESS (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) and QT shortening
records.NOTES: - Efficacy messaging needs balanced safety; a serious reaction like DRESS can't be omitted

records.SUMMARY:
- PharmaTher webpage for its generic ketamine injection implied broader therapeutic uses (such as mental health) beyond the approved indication
- As a 505(j) generic of Ketalar, ketamine carries the same narrow approved uses (sole anesthetic for certain procedures, induction, and supplemental anesthesia), so suggesting other uses misrepresents the indication, and the page also lacked risk information
- The webpage wasn't submitted to FDA on Form FDA-2253 as required, making it both misbranded and a procedural violation
records.NOTES: - A generic's promotion can't claim uses beyond the reference label's narrow indication, must carry risk information, and must be submitted on Form 2253

records.SUMMARY:
- Farxiga DTC TV ad's superimposed text said "FARXIGA can help you keep living life by reducing the risk of cardiovascular death," representing the drug as approved on that single endpoint in CKD or heart failure
- Misleading because the chronic kidney disease approval rested on a composite primary endpoint (>=50% sustained eGFR decline, end-stage kidney disease, and cardiovascular or renal death), so singling out cardiovascular death cherry-picks one component
- Created a misleading impression of the actual approved benefit
records.NOTES: - Pulling one component out of a composite endpoint overstates what the approval established; claims have to track the actual basis for approval

records.SUMMARY:
- Fabhalta 90-second DTC TV ad ran a romantic story arc (a man meeting a woman at the grocery store) that played through the major statement
- The attention-grabbing visuals, frequent scene changes, and emotional storyline distracted from the communication of risk information
- That undercut the boxed-warning safety message for a PNH drug (serious infections with encapsulated bacteria) by burying it under the narrative
records.NOTES: - An emotional storyline and busy visuals during the major statement interfere with comprehension of risk, especially a boxed warning

records.SUMMARY:
- Apretude slide deck promoted the long-acting injectable's HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis efficacy but presented it with no balancing risk and safety information
- Left out material risks from the label, such as hypersensitivity reactions, hepatotoxicity, and depressive disorders, breaking fair balance
- Benefit-only framing in an HCP piece created a misleading impression of the drug's safety
records.NOTES: - Efficacy slides still need balanced risk information; a benefit-only PrEP piece omitting labeled serious risks is misleading

records.SUMMARY:
- FYARRO presentation made broad efficacy claims for advanced malignant PEComa, a rare cancer, in a way that overstated the benefit
- The approval rests on a single-arm trial (response rate), so the materials implied a level of proven benefit the design can't establish
- Downplayed the serious labeled risks, such as stomatitis, infections, and pneumonitis
records.NOTES: - Single-arm response data can't support broad efficacy impressions, and rare-cancer promos still need balanced risk disclosure

records.SUMMARY:
- Anktiva promotional materials overstated efficacy for BCG-unresponsive bladder cancer, implying broad cancer benefit and durable cure or recurrence prevention
- The claims outran the approval data: QUILT-3.032 was a single-arm study, so it can't establish the implied long-term cancer-free outcomes
- Created a misleading impression of proven, broadly effective treatment; OPDP raised the same concerns again in January and March 2026
records.NOTES: - Cure and recurrence-prevention language overstates single-arm oncology data, and repeating flagged claims invites escalation from an untitled to a warning letter

records.SUMMARY:
- Iclusig brochure promoted the leukemia (CML / Ph+ ALL) benefits but didn't properly present the drug's serious risks
- Omitted or minimized boxed-warning safety information, including arterial occlusion, venous thromboembolism, heart failure, and hepatotoxicity
- The benefit-forward presentation created a misleading impression of the drug's safety
records.NOTES: - Boxed-warning risks like arterial occlusion can't be omitted from a benefit-focused brochure; fair balance is required

records.SUMMARY:
- Imdelltra promotion overstated how well the drug works for small cell lung cancer, suggesting benefits beyond what trials established
- The accelerated approval rests on response-rate data, so implied survival or durable benefit went past the evidence
- Underplayed serious labeled risks, including cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurologic toxicity (ICANS)
records.NOTES: - Accelerated-approval response data can't support survival or durable-benefit impressions, and CRS/neurotoxicity warnings can't be minimized

records.SUMMARY:
- Jeuveau materials promoted the cosmetic wrinkle (glabellar line) benefit with no required risk information
- The benefit-only presentation left the efficacy claims unbalanced
- Omitted material safety information, including the boxed warning for distant spread of toxin effect
records.NOTES: - Even cosmetic-toxin promotion has to carry risk information, including the boxed warning for distant spread of toxin effect

records.SUMMARY:
- Pemazyre piece claimed benefits in cholangiocarcinoma (FGFR2-fusion) without solid supporting evidence
- The accelerated approval rests on a single-arm trial (response rate), so the materials overstated proven benefit
- Played down serious labeled risks such as ocular toxicity (retinal pigment epithelial detachment) and hyperphosphatemia
records.NOTES: - Single-arm response data behind an accelerated approval can't support firm efficacy claims, and ocular and other serious risks must be disclosed

records.SUMMARY:
- Photofrin material made treatment claims for photodynamic therapy in cancer but omitted or barely mentioned the major risks
- Left out or minimized serious labeled risks, especially severe photosensitivity (patients must avoid sun and bright light) and respiratory and chest complications
- The benefit-forward presentation created a misleading impression of the drug's safety
records.NOTES: - A benefit-focused oncology piece can't omit defining risks like photosensitivity; fair balance is required regardless of channel

records.SUMMARY:
- Photofrin material made treatment claims for photodynamic therapy in cancer but omitted or barely mentioned the major risks
- Left out or minimized serious labeled risks, especially severe photosensitivity (patients must avoid sun and bright light) and respiratory and chest complications
- The benefit-forward presentation created a misleading impression of the drug's safety
records.NOTES: - A benefit-focused oncology piece can't omit defining risks like photosensitivity; fair balance is required regardless of channel

records.SUMMARY:
- Rystiggo content implied better efficacy or patient outcomes for generalized myasthenia gravis than the data support
- Overstated the consistency or durability of benefit beyond what the trial established
- Minimized or omitted serious labeled risks, such as aseptic meningitis, infections, and hypersensitivity reactions
records.NOTES: - Promotional materials can't imply outcomes beyond the trial, and serious risks like aseptic meningitis must be presented

records.SUMMARY:
- Skyrizi presentation pushed the plaque psoriasis efficacy (high skin-clearance rates) but left out important risk information from the label
- The efficacy-forward framing wasn't balanced against the drug's safety profile
- Omitted material risks such as serious infections (including tuberculosis) and hypersensitivity reactions
records.NOTES: - High-clearance efficacy claims still require balanced risk disclosure; infection and hypersensitivity risks can't be dropped

records.SUMMARY:
- Tazverik content made broad benefit claims for epithelioid sarcoma without solid supporting evidence
- The accelerated approval rests on single-arm response data, so the materials overstated proven benefit
- Downplayed the safety profile, including the labeled risk of secondary malignancies
records.NOTES: - Single-arm response data can't carry broad efficacy claims, and a serious risk like secondary malignancy can't be downplayed

records.SUMMARY:
- Amvuttra DTC TV ad implied patients could be "carefree" about the effects of their cardiomyopathy
- That overstated benefit: the trial data didn't support an impression of worry-free disease control for a progressive, life-threatening condition
- Lacked adequate support for the lifestyle impression and didn't properly convey the seriousness of the disease; Alnylam pulled the ad after the letter
records.NOTES: - "Carefree" lifestyle framing implies a level of control the data don't support, especially for a serious progressive disease

records.SUMMARY:
- Acoramidis (Attruby) material made efficacy claims hinting at better ATTR-CM outcomes than were established
- Implied benefits the data didn't reliably support, and the drug wasn't fully approved at the time of the promotion
- Created a misleading impression of proven benefit ahead of an established, labeled efficacy basis
records.NOTES: - Pre-approval or unsupported outcome claims overstate what's established; promotion has to track approved labeling

records.SUMMARY:
- Austedo XR communications overstated how much patients improve with tardive dyskinesia
- Implied a level of symptom control beyond what the trials established
- Minimized known risks, including the boxed warning for depression and suicidality (in Huntington's disease) and QT prolongation
records.NOTES: - Overstated symptom improvement plus a minimized boxed-warning depression/suicidality risk is a fair-balance failure

records.SUMMARY:
- Austedo XR communications overstated how much patients improve with tardive dyskinesia
- Implied a level of symptom control beyond what the trials established
- Minimized known risks, including the boxed warning for depression and suicidality (in Huntington's disease) and QT prolongation
records.NOTES: - Overstated symptom improvement plus a minimized boxed-warning depression/suicidality risk is a fair-balance failure

records.SUMMARY:
- Auvelity material talked up fast benefits and remission rates that overstate how well it works for major depressive disorder
- Implied rapid, broad efficacy beyond what the data reliably support
- Didn't properly balance the risks, including the boxed warning for suicidal thoughts and behaviors and the seizure risk
records.NOTES: - Fast-onset and remission claims need solid support, and a boxed-warning suicidality risk can't be downplayed

records.SUMMARY:
- Bimzelx visual aids presented unbalanced efficacy information for plaque psoriasis, leaning on high skin-clearance rates
- Benefit claims weren't balanced with proper risk disclosure
- Omitted or minimized serious labeled risks, such as infections (including fungal/candida and tuberculosis) and hepatotoxicity
records.NOTES: - High-clearance efficacy can't be presented without balanced risk information; infection and liver risks belong alongside the claims

records.SUMMARY:
- Bimzelx visual aids presented unbalanced efficacy information for plaque psoriasis, leaning on high skin-clearance rates
- Benefit claims weren't balanced with proper risk disclosure
- Omitted or minimized serious labeled risks, such as infections (including fungal/candida and tuberculosis) and hepatotoxicity
records.NOTES: - High-clearance efficacy can't be presented without balanced risk information; infection and liver risks belong alongside the claims

records.SUMMARY:
- Calquence materials implied it beats other BTK inhibitors for CLL, suggesting better survival or safety
- Leaned on indirect, cross-trial comparisons rather than head-to-head evidence, so the superiority impression wasn't supported
- Created a misleading comparative claim about efficacy and safety
records.NOTES: - Comparative superiority claims need head-to-head data; indirect cross-trial comparisons can't support "better than" messaging

records.SUMMARY:
- Camzyos communications hyped symptom and outcome improvements for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in broad terms
- Overstated the benefit beyond what the trials established
- Didn't adequately convey serious risks, including the boxed warning for heart failure and the REMS requirements
records.NOTES: - Broad outcome claims have to track the data, and a boxed-warning heart-failure risk plus REMS context can't be omitted

records.SUMMARY:
- Contrave consumer brochure overstated weight-loss results and suggested broad use beyond the approved population
- Implied stronger or more general efficacy than the data support
- Left out serious risks, including the boxed warning for suicidal thoughts and behaviors, plus seizure and blood-pressure increases
records.NOTES: - Weight-loss efficacy claims must stay within the approved use, and the boxed-warning neuropsychiatric risk can't be omitted

records.SUMMARY:
- Promoted Danziten (a nilotinib formulation) for CML, suggesting leukemia benefits without proper balancing risk information
- Benefit claims ran ahead of the approved, labeled basis and lacked required context
- Omitted serious safety information, including the boxed warning for QT prolongation and risk of sudden death
records.NOTES: - Leukemia benefit claims need balanced risk disclosure, and a boxed warning like QT prolongation can't be left out

records.SUMMARY:
- Fasenra consumer promotion talked up asthma symptom improvement with no required risk information
- The benefit-only framing left the efficacy claims unbalanced
- Skipped serious labeled reactions, including hypersensitivity reactions such as anaphylaxis
records.NOTES: - Consumer asthma promos still need balanced risk disclosure; hypersensitivity reactions can't be left out
