records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Misleading efficacy / misleading representation of evidence
records.SUMMARY:
-Presented OS and PFS data in a way that suggested drug-driven outcomes despite single-arm trial design
-Used graphs and statistics that made results appear more definitive and interpretable than they actually are
-Claimed drug was a “preferred” NCCN  treatment in a way that overstated guideline recommendations
records.NOTES: -Single arm trials mean OS and PFS data can't prove the drig actually cuased the outcomes, even if the result looks strong

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Misleading efficacy / misleading representation of evidence
records.SUMMARY:
-Fruzzqla "Quality of Life" wepage suggested the drig helps preserve QoL based on Fresco-2 Data
-Presented PRO results in a way that QoL benefits seem proven, even though the data was exploratory and not statistically reliable
-Highlighed symptom stability and delayed worsening in a way that overstated the drug's impact on overall QoL
records.NOTES: -PRO and Qol data had major design limits, so it can't support strong efficiveness claims

records.SUMMARY:
-Ingrezza TV ad showed near complete resoluition of TD symptoms, overstating how much patients actually improve
-Claimed rapid improvement at 2 weeks using exploratory data, even though the main study was at 6 weeks and ealier resluts weren't reliable
-Presented "98% patient satisfation" in a way that made it seem broadly applicable, even though the data came from a selective/enriched group of patients
records.NOTES: - Using exploratory endpoints and selective patient populations can make efficacy results look stronger and more generalizable than they actually are

records.SUMMARY:
-Kesimpta TV ad claimed the treatment can be taken in “1 minute a month,” oversimplifying the actual administration process and required steps
 - Downplayed important details like the need for first-dose guidance from a healthcare provider, making the treatment seem easier and faster than it is
 - Used distracting visuals during the major statement that pulled attention away from the risk information
records.NOTES: - Oversimplifying administration and using distracting visuals can make treatment seem easier and safer than it actually is while reducing understanding of risk information

records.SUMMARY:
- Jardiance TV ad used fast-paced dancing and movie-themed visuals that distracted from the communication of benefits and limitations of use
- Competing visuals, music, and scene changes made it harder for viewers to understand key information about the drug’s effectiveness
- Risk information was hard to read due to font size, placement, and ongoing distractions on screen
records.NOTES: - Distracting visuals and poor text readability can interfere with understanding of both benefit and risk information, creating a misleading overall impression

records.SUMMARY:
-Jardiance TV ad used fast-paced dancing and softball-themed visuals that distracted from the communication of benefits and limitations of use
- Competing visuals, music, and frequent scene changes made it harder for viewers to understand key effectiveness information
- Risk information was difficult to read due to poor font size, placement, and ongoing on-screen distraction
records.NOTES: - Distractions and poor readability during the major statement can reduce understanding of both risk and benefit information, leading to a misleading overall impression

records.SUMMARY:
-- Jardiance TV ad used fast-paced dancing and supermarket-themed visuals that distracted from the communication of benefits and limitations of use
- Competing visuals, music, and frequent scene changes made it harder for viewers to understand key effectiveness information
- Risk information was difficult to read due to poor font size, placement, and ongoing on-screen distractions
records.NOTES: - Distracting visuals and poor text readability during the major statement can interfere with understanding of risk and benefit information, creating a misleading impression

records.SUMMARY:
- Jublia TV ad showed a patient’s nail fungus completely resolving after treatment, implying a guaranteed or complete cure
 - Used messaging like “don’t fear the fungus” that made the condition seem easy to treat and minimized the actual variability in outcomes
 - Suggested near-immediate results, even though treatment requires long-term use (up to 48 weeks) and cure rates are relatively low
records.NOTES: - Ads that show fast or complete resolution can overpromise outcomes when real-world cure rates are limited and require long treatment durations

records.SUMMARY:
- Kisqali TV ad claimed the drug helps patients live longer using OS (Overall Survival) data from an exploratory analysis, making the benefit seem more certain than it is
 - Presented survival data without proper context, even though the exploratory analysis was not pre-specified and may be unreliable
 - Included key efficacy information in a fast-moving SUPER (on-screen text) that was too dense and fast for viewers to read and understand
records.NOTES: - Using exploratory survival data without clear context and presenting it too quickly can mislead viewers about the true strength of evidence

records.SUMMARY:
- Linzess TV ad used frequent scene changes and upbeat family visuals during the major statement that distracted from risk information
 - Emotional and engaging visuals competed for attention while safety information was being presented 
- Overall presentation made it harder for viewers to hear, read, and understand the major statement
records.NOTES: - Attention-grabbing or emotional visuals during the major statement can reduce comprehension of risk information and create a misleading impression

records.SUMMARY:
- Neffy TV ad claimed patients can say “goodbye to needles,” suggesting they would no longer need injectable treatments for allergic emergencies
 - Implied the product eliminates needle fear and emotional concerns, despite no evidence supporting this type of benefit 
- Suggested patients could freely engage in activities (like eating risky foods) without worry, overstating the level of protection the drug provides
records.NOTES: - Ads that imply reduced need for caution or complete freedom from risk can mislead patients about appropriate safety behaviors, especially for serious conditions like anaphylaxis

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Misleading efficacy / misleading representation of evidence
records.SUMMARY:
- Nemluvio TV ad suggested the drug can be used alone, failing to clearly communicate it must be used with topical treatments
 - Claimed rapid itch relief within 48 hours using post-hoc (after-the-fact) data that wasn’t pre-specified or statistically reliable
 - Suggested many patients achieve and maintain clear skin, overstating results when actual response rates were limited
records.NOTES: - Using post-hoc data and not clearly stating combination use can make efficacy look stronger and more broadly applicable than supported by the evidence

records.SUMMARY:
- Opdivo Qvantig TV ad emphasized fast 3-minute treatment, suggesting patients spend minimal time in clinical settings 
- Visuals showed patients enjoying activities instead of being in treatment, overstating convenience and real-world experience
 - Frequent scene changes and engaging visuals during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.NOTES: - Ads that overemphasize convenience and lifestyle benefits can mislead patients about the actual treatment experience and time required

records.SUMMARY:
- Opdivo + Yervoy TV ad showed patients living active, unrestricted lives, suggesting they can maintain normal functioning during treatment
 - Implied improvements in HRQoL, including emotional and social well-being, without supporting evidence
 - Attention-grabbing visuals and frequent scene changes during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.NOTES: - Ads that portray patients as fully functional or improved in QoL without evidence can mislead expectations about real treatment experience

records.SUMMARY:
- Qelbree TV ad showed complete resolution of ADHD symptoms, overstating the level of improvement seen in clinical studies
 - Portrayed patients as fully symptom-free, even though actual score improvements were more modest
 - Used frequent scene changes and positive visuals during the major statement, which distracted from and minimized the seriousness of risks
records.NOTES: - Ads that show full symptom resolution and pair risk info with upbeat visuals can overstate efficacy and downplay safety concerns

records.SUMMARY:
- Qelbree TV ad showed complete resolution of ADHD symptoms, overstating the level of improvement seen in clinical studies
- Portrayed patients as fully functional and symptom-free, even though actual improvements were more modest
- Used frequent scene changes and upbeat visuals during the major statement, distracting from and minimizing the communication of risks
records.NOTES: - Showing full symptom resolution and pairing risk info with positive visuals can overstate effectiveness and downplay safety concerns

records.SUMMARY:
- Qulipta TV ad used a before-and-after storyline that suggested complete prevention of migraines, overstating the drug’s actual effect
- Messaging like “more zero-migraine days” and “forget-you-get migraine medicine” implied patients would stop having migraines entirely
- Attention-grabbing visuals and scene changes during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.NOTES: - Ads that imply total symptom elimination (like zero migraines) can mislead when clinical data only shows modest reductions in migraine days

records.SUMMARY:
- Pluvicto TV ad used storytelling and success visuals that suggested the drug would lead to positive outcomes despite prior treatment failures 
- Omitted key parts of the approved indication, making it seem appropriate for a broader patient population than actually approved
 - Used frequent scene changes and engaging visuals during the major statement, distracting from the communication of risk information
records.NOTES: - Omitting key indication limits and implying guaranteed success can make a treatment seem more broadly effective than supported by clinical data

records.SUMMARY:
- Truqap TV ad used frequent scene changes and visually engaging transitions during the major statement that distracted from risk information 
- Creative visuals and storytelling elements competed for viewer attention while safety information was being presented
- Overall presentation made it harder for viewers to process and understand the major statement
records.NOTES: - Complex visuals and frequent transitions during the major statement can interfere with comprehension of important safety information

records.SUMMARY:
- Tzield TV ad suggested the drug can improve QoL and emotional well-being beyond delaying disease progression
- Messaging implied patients could “win” against type 1 diabetes, overstating the drug’s actual benefit
- Frequent scene changes and attention-grabbing visuals during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.NOTES: - Claims implying improved QoL or “winning” a disease can mislead when the drug is only shown to delay progression, not improve overall functioning

records.SUMMARY:
- Velsipity TV ad used a visual “creature” to represent ulcerative colitis, showing a dramatic transformation that overstated treatment effectiveness 
- Depicted near-complete control of symptoms, even though clinical remission rates were relatively limited
 - The same visuals during the major statement distracted from and interfered with the communication of risk information
records.NOTES: - Exaggerated visual metaphors can make modest clinical improvements appear like complete symptom control, misleading patients about expected results

records.SUMMARY:
- Voquezna interview video featured a spokesperson discussing the drug’s benefits and personal experience
 - Encouraged use and directed viewers to seek more information, but did not include any risk or safety information
 - Completely omitted risk disclosures, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
records.NOTES: - Any promotional content that includes benefit claims must also include risk information, even in interviews or spokesperson-led content

records.SUMMARY:
- Voquezna interview video featured a spokesperson discussing GERD and use of the drug
 - Included benefit-related messaging and personal experience claims without any safety or risk information
 - Completely omitted risk disclosures, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
records.NOTES: - Even informal content like interviews must include risk information when benefit claims are mentioned

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / failure to submit
records.SUMMARY:
- Voquezna interview video featured a spokesperson discussing personal experience and benefits of the drug 
- Included statements suggesting effectiveness and encouraging use, but directed viewers elsewhere for more information instead of presenting it directly 
- Completely omitted risk disclosures, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
records.NOTES: - Pointing viewers to a website or doctor does not replace the need to include risk information alongside benefit claims

records.SUMMARY:
- Ultomiris TV ad claimed “continuous symptom control” and strong improvements in daily activities, overstating the drug’s actual demonstrated benefit
 - Presented outcome measures in a way that implied broader and consistent control than supported by the data 
- Frequent scene changes and engaging visuals during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.NOTES: - Framing limited clinical endpoints (like MG-ADL – Myasthenia Gravis Activities of Daily Living) as continuous or complete control can overstate real-world effectiveness

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Misleading safety comparisons / risk minimization / unsupported comparative claims
records.SUMMARY:
- Nextstellis speaker deck suggested the drug is safer than other CHCs  using unsupported “low-impact” estrogen claims 
- Used comparative charts and statistical references (like p-values) to imply safety advantages that were not backed by evidence 
- Minimized and omitted serious risks (e.g., liver effects, thromboembolic risk, breast cancer risk) and misrepresented adverse event data
records.NOTES: - Comparative safety claims require strong evidence; selective data and misused statistics can mislead HCP decision-making

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Misleading efficacy / misleading representation of evidence
records.SUMMARY:
- Presented PFS and OS data from a single-arm trial in a way that suggested survival benefit without a comparator  
- Promoted DCR including stable disease even though it cannot be attributed to the drug in a single-arm study  
- Used study results not capable of supporting efficacy conclusions, and disclaimers did not fix the misleading impression
records.NOTES: - Single-arm trials cannot support PFS, OS, or DCR claims tied to drug effect, and including stable disease can overstate efficacy

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.SUMMARY:
- Presented benefit and convenience claims while omitting all risk information, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety despite multiple known serious risks
- Highlighted reduced tablet burden and bioequivalence without providing any corresponding safety context
- Used a flawed real-world study to suggest improved adherence versus generic dexamethasone, despite major design issues that make the comparison unreliable
records.NOTES: Omitting all risk information alongside benefit claims is a major violation; weak or biased study design cannot support comparative adherence or efficacy claims

records.SUMMARY:
- Presented benefits prominently in an influencer-style video while risk information was delayed, hard to read, and easy to miss, minimizing serious safety risks
 - Suggested rapid or same-day treatment effects (getting ready for a “big night out”), which is not supported by clinical data
 - Implied product superiority (“smart tox” / manufacturing advantage) without evidence comparing it to other botulinum toxin products
records.NOTES: Social media and influencer content must present risk information clearly and alongside benefits; fast, hidden, or delayed risk disclosures and unsupported superiority claims are misleading

records.SUMMARY:
- Claimed that one dose “quickly eliminates migraine pain,” overstating both the speed and extent of benefit compared to clinical data
- Used strong before-and-after visuals suggesting rapid and complete symptom relief, which is not supported by study results
- Implied that most or all patients achieve pain elimination after one dose, even though only a minority of patients achieved pain freedom in trials
records.NOTES: - Claims of rapid or complete symptom elimination must match clinical trial endpoints; using strong visuals and celebrity endorsements can amplify misleading efficacy impressions

records.SUMMARY:
- Krazati HCP website overstated how well it works using made-up composite measures and single-arm data
- Implied stuff like an 80% disease control rate and longer survival that wasn't actually shown
records.NOTES: - Krazati got accelerated approval, so pulling survival claims from a single-arm trial is misleading

records.SUMMARY:
- Paid a celebrity to promote Auvi-Q on Instagram to parents of kids with allergies
- Post only talked benefits and had zero risk info
records.NOTES: - Dropped a link for safety info instead of actually saying the risks in the post

records.SUMMARY:
- Claimed Kisqali “preserves quality of life” and patients are “living well,” based on exploratory PRO data that cannot support definitive conclusions
- Suggested survival benefits (“live longer,” “long live life”) in a way that overstated or miscommunicated the actual clinical data
- Presented important qualifying information in a fast, hard-to-read SUPER with distracting visuals and audio, reducing viewers’ ability to understand the true efficacy results
records.NOTES: - Exploratory or secondary endpoints (like QoL PROs) cannot support strong claims, and key data in SUPERs must be readable and not undermined by distracting visuals

records.SUMMARY: - Claimed Rexulti provides a “62% greater reduction” in depression symptoms, which significantly overstates the actual treatment effect - Misrepresented clinical data by using a percentage that exaggerates the real improvement compared to baseline and placebo differences - Implied patients would experience a large reduction in symptoms, when actual data showed only modest improvement
records.NOTES: - Misusing percentages or statistics to inflate treatment effects is misleading, especially when actual clinical improvements are much smaller

records.SUMMARY:
- Claimed Phexxi prevents pregnancy 99% of the time using a “per act of sex” analysis, which overstates effectiveness 
- Used an invalid methodology that ignores variation in pregnancy likelihood across the menstrual cycle (fertile window)
 - Misrepresented efficacy compared to approved labeling, which shows lower real
world pregnancy prevention rates
records.NOTES: - Using non-standard or unsupported efficacy metrics (like per-act analysis) can inflate results and mislead about real-world effectiveness

records.SUMMARY:
- Presented benefit claims but omitted all risk information, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
 - Claimed patients would have “periods on a schedule,” overstating predictability of bleeding patterns not supported by clinical data
- Ignored evidence of irregular and unscheduled bleeding, which is common with the drug and contradicts the claim
records.NOTES: - Social media posts must include risk information alongside benefits, and claims about predictable outcomes must reflect real clinical variability

records.SUMMARY:
- Overstated efficacy by claiming 67% of patients achieved normal cortisol levels, using an early study phase instead of final results 
- Presented efficacy results from a selected subset of patients, implying outcomes represent the general population 
- Minimized serious risks by using vague language and suggesting monitoring could prevent side effects, despite boxed warnings and potentially fatal risks
records.NOTES: - Using early-phase or enriched population data can inflate perceived effectiveness, and vague risk language cannot downplay serious boxed warning risks

records.SUMMARY:
- Claimed large LDL-C reductions by combining results from separate studies, using a scientifically unsupported cross-study calculation 
- Suggested specific treatment effects and target outcomes (e.g., LDL-C <70 mg/dL) that were not reliably supported by clinical trial design or endpoints 
- Minimized risk information by presenting benefits prominently while relegating serious risks to less visible, harder-to-read sections
records.NOTES: - Combining results across different studies to create efficacy claims is not valid, and risk information must be presented with equal prominence to benefits

records.SUMMARY:
- Omitted important safety information in the video, including serious risks like embryofetal risk and photosensitivity, while presenting strong benefit claims
- Suggested product superiority and unique status (“first and only”) and implied effectiveness in patients who failed other treatments, without supporting evidence
- Used post hoc analyses and manipulated data (e.g., removing vehicle effect) to claim “synergy” and greater efficacy, overstating the drug’s actual benefit
records.NOTES: - Post hoc analyses and selective data presentation cannot support strong claims, and risk information must be clearly presented alongside benefit claims

records.SUMMARY: - Highlighted benefit claim (A1C reduction) but failed to adequately communicate the full indication and key limitations of use - Presented risk information in small, fast-scrolling text that was difficult to read and overshadowed by distracting visuals and audio - Omitted important risk details (e.g., hypoglycemia risk with other medications), creating a misleading impression about safety
records.NOTES: - Risk and indication information must be clearly presented and readable; fast-paced or hidden disclosures do not fix misleading impressions

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / failure to submit
records.SUMMARY:
- Two TV commercials for Emgality made promotional claims but left out all the risk info
- No major side effects or safety warnings shown at all
records.NOTES: - Ads were never submitted to FDA on Form 2253 like they're supposed to be

records.SUMMARY:
- Presented benefit and use claims in sponsored search links without including any risk information
- Highlighted dosing flexibility and pediatric convenience without communicating serious safety risks
- Created a misleading impression of safety by omitting risk information for a drug used in a vulnerable pediatric population
records.NOTES: - Even short formats like search ads must include risk information when making benefit claims, especially for drugs with serious risks

records.SUMMARY:
- Claimed higher risk of febrile neutropenia with prefilled syringe vs Onpro device based on a real-world study with significant design limitations 
- Used an unvalidated algorithm and uncontrolled patient differences, making the comparative results unreliable 
- Presented relative risk differences prominently (31%) despite very small absolute differences and potential bias, overstating clinical significance
records.NOTES: - Comparative claims require well-controlled evidence; flawed real-world studies with bias and small absolute differences cannot support superiority claims

records.SUMMARY:
- Claimed rapid relief within 15–30 minutes based on a spokesperson testimonial, despite no clinical data supporting efficacy at that timeframe 
- Suggested superiority over other treatments (e.g., “game changer,” no rebound headaches), without supporting comparative evidence 
- Minimized risk information by presenting it briefly, in small text at the end of the video, and failed to clearly communicate indication limitations
records.NOTES: - Celebrity testimonials cannot support clinical claims, and personal experiences cannot be used to imply rapid onset or superiority without evidence

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.SUMMARY:
- Presented benefit claims prominently while omitting important warnings and precautions beyond the boxed warning 
- Failed to include key safety risk regarding improper dosing, which can lead to fatal toxicity 
- Did not adequately communicate the full indication, omitting that the drug is for pediatric patients and part of combination therapy
records.NOTES: - Including only a boxed warning is not sufficient; all material risks must be included, and indications must be fully and clearly stated

records.SUMMARY:
- Omitted important risk information, including hypersensitivity reactions and serious consequences of contraindications
- Claimed the product is “easy to use” and users would know they “did it right,” overstating ease and confidence without supporting evidence
- Failed to clearly communicate proper use context, suggesting it can be used in mild situations rather than severe hypoglycemia requiring caregiver assistance
records.NOTES: - Ease-of-use claims must reflect actual administration complexity, especially for emergency-use products, and proper use context must be clearly explained

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.SUMMARY:
- Presented benefit claims without any risk information, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
- Claimed Triferic is safer and more effective than IV iron products without supporting comparative evidence
- Failed to include full indication and limitations of use, suggesting broader applicability than approved
records.NOTES: - Unsupported superiority claims and omission of key indication limits can mislead providers about both effectiveness and appropriate use

records.SUMMARY:
- Promoted an investigational drug as safe and effective before FDA approval, including claims that it “cures” brain cancer 
- Made strong efficacy and survival benefit claims without established clinical evidence 
- Failed to clearly disclose that the drug is investigational and not approved for commercial use
records.NOTES: - Investigational drugs cannot be promoted as safe or effective; any claims before approval are considered pre-approval promotion and are prohibited

records.SUMMARY:
- Used patient testimonials to suggest reduced or no side effects compared to other statins, implying improved safety without supporting evidence
- Created a misleading impression of superiority by showing patients switching from other statins due to side effects and improving on Livalo
- Minimized risk communication by presenting safety information as scrolling text that was difficult to read and overshadowed by testimonials
records.NOTES: - Patient testimonials cannot be used to imply reduced side effects or superiority without evidence, and risk information must be presented clearly and prominently

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.SUMMARY:
- Omitted key risk information, including important contraindications and serious risks like pelvic infection and device expulsion
- Presented risk information in a way that was difficult to understand due to fast-paced visuals, music, and competing distractions
- Repeated “hormone-free” claims that minimized perceived risk and suggested improved safety compared to other contraceptives
records.NOTES: - Even accurate claims (e.g., “hormone-free”) can be misleading if they imply improved safety or distract from serious risks

records.SUMMARY:
- Omitted serious risks such as potential eye injury and severe skin reactions, minimizing the drug’s safety concerns
- Suggested typical outcomes of complete lesion clearance using before-and-after images, overstating effectiveness compared to clinical data
- Presented limited side effect information (e.g., stinging only) while failing to disclose the full range and duration of common adverse reactions
records.NOTES: - Before-and-after visuals and testimonials cannot be used to imply typical outcomes or downplay risks without corresponding clinical evidence

records.SUMMARY:
- Used patient testimonials to suggest reduced or no side effects compared to other statins, implying improved safety without supporting evidence
- Created a misleading impression of superiority by showing patients switching from other statins and experiencing fewer side effects
- Minimized risk communication by presenting safety information as scrolling text that was difficult to read and overshadowed by testimonial content
records.NOTES: - Testimonials cannot be used to imply improved tolerability or safety without evidence, and risk information must be presented clearly and prominently

records.SUMMARY:
- Promoted an investigational imaging agent as effective and clinically useful for detecting prostate cancer recurrence before FDA approval
- Made comparative claims suggesting better performance than approved imaging agents without established evidence
- Failed to clearly communicate that the product is investigational, creating a misleading impression of approval and clinical use
records.NOTES: - Investigational products cannot be promoted or compared to approved therapies as safe or effective prior to FDA approval

records.SUMMARY:
- Promoted Fycompa for unapproved uses, suggesting safety and effectiveness beyond its FDA-approved indication
- Provided statements indicating use in populations or settings not yet approved at the time
- Minimized serious boxed warning risks (psychiatric and behavioral effects), downplaying concerns and discouraging caution
records.NOTES: - Sales representatives cannot promote off-label uses or downplay boxed warnings; verbal promotion is held to the same standards as formal materials

records.SUMMARY:
- Claimed EstroGel provides the “lowest effective dose” of estrogen, implying superiority over other estrogen products
- Suggested no other therapies offer lower effective doses, which is not supported by available FDA-approved products
- Relied on studies that did not compare EstroGel to other estrogen therapies, making the comparative claim unsupported
records.NOTES: - Claims of “lowest dose” or similar superiority require direct comparative evidence; indirect or unrelated studies cannot support such claims

records.SUMMARY:
- Promoted an investigational drug as effective for treating AML, including claims of mutation eradication and combination use with chemotherapy
- Suggested clinical benefit and superiority versus other therapies without established evidence
- Failed to clearly disclose that the product is investigational and not FDA-approved, creating a misleading impression of established use
records.NOTES: - Investigational drugs cannot be promoted as safe, effective, or superior, especially in venues like conferences where they may appear alongside approved products

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.SUMMARY:
- Completely omitted all risk information, despite the drug having boxed warnings for serious conditions (e.g., cancer and cardiovascular risks)
- Used patient testimony suggesting no side effects, reinforcing a misleading impression of safety
- Claimed “instant relief” of symptoms, which is not supported by clinical evidence
records.NOTES: - Omission of all risk information, especially for drugs with boxed warnings, is a major violation, and patient testimonials cannot replace required safety disclosures

records.SUMMARY:
- Emphasized abuse-deterrent properties while failing to adequately communicate serious risks, including addiction, abuse, overdose, and death
- Omitted key limitations of use, particularly that the drug should only be used when alternative treatments are inadequate
- Presented qualifying information about limitations of abuse-deterrent properties in a way that was not visible or prominent, undermining risk communication
records.NOTES: - Highlighting abuse-deterrent features can be misleading if it implies the drug is safer; opioids still carry significant risks regardless of formulation

records.SUMMARY:
- Promoted an investigational imaging drug as safe and effective even though it was not FDA-approved
- Made strong claims about accuracy, superiority, and patient benefit without established evidence
- Suggested no significant risks and improved outcomes, which are not allowed for unapproved products
records.NOTES: - Investigational drugs cannot be promoted as safe or effective before FDA approval; even strong preliminary claims are considered improper promotion

records.VIOLATION CATEGORY: Omission of risk information / misleading presentation
records.SUMMARY:
- Included limited risk statements but omitted other serious contraindications and boxed warning details, creating a misleading impression of safety
- Failed to adequately communicate neuropsychiatric risks and other serious safety concerns
- Presented risk information in SUPERs only or alongside competing audio messages, reducing clarity and comprehension
records.NOTES: - Partial risk disclosure is not sufficient; missing key risks or presenting them unclearly can still mislead about overall safety

records.WHAT THEY SAID:
“There’s a lot to consider when finding the best CLL [chronic lymphocytic leukemia] treatment for you. Brukinsa brings clarity to your path ahead”
“See your future clearly again,”
records.WHY FDA FLAGGED IT: FDA has determined that the TV ad is false or misleading

records.WHAT THEY SAID:
“Heart disease. Blockage of the arteries in the heart (ischemic heart disease) that can lead to death has happened in some people during treatment with NUBEQA”
“Nubeqa may cause serious side effects including heart disease and seizure”
“What are the possible side effects of NUBEQA?”
records.WHY FDA FLAGGED IT: The video and TV ad are misleading because they include claims and representations about the benefits of Nubeqa but omit important risk information associated with the drug

records.WHAT THEY SAID:
“Niktimvo may offer fast and lasting responses”

records.Allegation Summary: - Sandoz challenged the claim that the Vicks syrup "starts to work the instant you swallow," arguing the FDA's failure to classify demulcents as effective made the claim per se false
records.Compliance Lesson: You can't win a Lanham Act suit just by pointing at FDA rules. The ad itself has to be provably false or misleading.
records.Notes:
- The court refused to let Sandoz use the Lanham Act to enforce the FDCA, since only the government can enforce that statute
- Drew the line courts still use today: "unsubstantiated under FDA standards" is not the same as "false" under the Lanham Act, and the plaintiff carries the burden of proving actual falsity
- Practical effect: competitors can't shortcut a false advertising case by borrowing FDA's approval framework

records.Allegation Summary: - Barr claimed ads calling the defendant's drug "generic" and "bioequivalent" falsely implied FDA approval
records.Compliance Lesson: Implied FDA-approval theories are weak. Courts want an affirmative false statement, not an inference.
records.Notes:
- The court found the ads never actually stated or positively suggested FDA approval, so nothing in them was literally false
- Shows how much heavier the burden is for implied claims: the plaintiff needs consumer perception evidence (surveys) proving the audience actually took away the false message
- Contrast with Zeneca and Novartis below, where implied messages did stick because the necessary implication was unavoidable

records.Products: Mylanta vs. Extra Strength Maalox Plus
records.Allegation Summary: - J&J challenged commercials calling Maalox "the strongest antacid there is," arguing lab-measured acid neutralizing capacity doesn't translate to better real-world relief
records.Outcome: Strength claims allowed with proper support
records.Compliance Lesson: Potency and strength claims must match what the underlying data actually measured, and nothing more.
records.Notes:
- The fight was over whether ANC (a lab measure) could support a consumer-facing "strongest" claim implying superior relief
- The Third Circuit worked through when a literally true claim (highest ANC) can still be misleading if consumers hear something different (works better)
- The irony: J&J argued the skeptical side here, then got hit with the same theory as a defendant in the 2002 Novartis Mylanta case below

records.Allegation Summary: - Braintree alleged a competitor took the same active ingredient as its approved kidney drug and falsely marketed it as a "dietary supplement" to skip the approval process
records.Compliance Lesson: When the real dispute is whether a product is a drug or a supplement, courts punt to FDA. Don't expect the Lanham Act to police approval status.
records.Notes:
- Classic primary jurisdiction ruling: deciding the claim would have required the court to first decide the product's regulatory classification, which is FDA's call
- Important for Medical Affairs because it shows the boundary between competitor litigation and regulatory complaints; the right move here was a citizen petition, not a lawsuit
- Post-POM Wonderful (2014), courts allow more FDCA-adjacent Lanham claims, but classification disputes like this one still get deferred

records.Allegation Summary: - Schering alleged Pfizer and its co-promoter's sales reps made false oral statements to physicians during detailing visits about a competing drug
records.Compliance Lesson: What reps say out loud in an exam room is advertising. Companies own their field force's words, and surveys can prove them.
records.Notes:
- Judge Sotomayor's opinion held physician surveys recording what reps said were admissible as evidence of the reps' statements, not hearsay, because you can't subpoena thousands of one-on-one conversations
- This is THE case making oral detailing claims actionable at scale, which is why promotional speaker decks and call scripts get compliance review
- Direct link to your DOJ sheet: the same field-force conduct that triggers Lanham suits from competitors is what shows up in FCA off-label settlements
records.Era: Timeline is a static image. If you add cases, ask for a re-render. Live counts stay in the table headers above.

records.Allegation Summary:
- Zeneca alleged Lilly reps promoted Evista as effective for reducing breast cancer risk when only Nolvadex had clinical trial data proving that use
- Lilly reps at times urged doctors to switch patients off tamoxifen
records.Compliance Lesson: Off-label promotion isn't just an FDA problem. The competitor whose approved indication you're poaching can sue and win an injunction.
records.Notes:
- Evista was approved only for osteoporosis; the breast cancer risk-reduction claim was an unapproved use, so the court treated the superiority messaging as false
- The opinion has a detailed record of rep training materials and incentive structures that pushed the off-label message, a good case study in how promotional intent gets proven
- Shows why competitive intelligence teams watch rival launch campaigns: the fastest enforcement often comes from the competitor, not OPDP

records.Products: Maalox vs. Mylanta Night Time Strength
records.Legal Basis: Lanham Act 43(a); NJ Consumer Fraud Act
records.Allegation Summary:
- Novartis alleged the "Night Time Strength" name and ads falsely implied special formulation for nighttime heartburn and all-night relief
- J&J had no data showing the product was specially formulated or worked better at night
records.Outcome: Injunction affirmed; J&J enjoined from using the name
records.Compliance Lesson: A claim with zero substantiation behind it is treated as false. Even the product's name counts as an advertising claim.

records.Allegation Summary: - Pharmacia challenged GSK TV commercials comparing nicotine replacement products, alleging false superiority and usage claims
records.Outcome: Injunction granted in part, denied in part
records.Compliance Lesson: Comparative OTC campaigns are two-way risk. Expect your rival to sue back, and expect courts to go claim by claim.
records.Notes:
- The court applied Novartis's unsubstantiated-claims framework and split the baby: some ad claims survived, others were enjoined
- Good illustration that Lanham analysis is claim-by-claim, not campaign-by-campaign, which is why claim matrices in promo review map each individual statement to its support
- The nicotine replacement category generated years of tit-for-tat suits, a pattern that repeats in every crowded OTC category

records.Allegation Summary: - Co-marketers of Actonel alleged Boniva ads misled physicians and consumers by implying nonspinal fracture protection that Boniva's trials never proved
records.Compliance Lesson: Don't blur proven and unproven endpoints. Claiming a benefit your trial wasn't powered to show is the classic comparative-ad trap.
records.Notes:
- The core allegation was endpoint inflation: Boniva had spinal fracture data but the marketing allegedly implied broader fracture protection
- Mirrors exactly what OPDP cites in your Untitled Letters sheet as Misleading Efficacy, showing competitor suits and FDA letters police the same conduct through different channels
- Also notable for the co-promotion wrinkle: two plaintiffs (P&G and Sanofi-Aventis) suing two defendants (Roche and GSK), since both products were co-marketed

records.Legal Basis: Lanham Act 43(a); trademark dilution; Mass. ch. 93A
records.Allegation Summary:
- Ferring alleged Braintree's comparison detailer and "Clean Freak" campaign misled doctors about Prepopik
- Braintree counterclaimed that Ferring's own ads were false, plus trade secret claims
records.Outcome: Most claims narrowed at summary judgment
records.Compliance Lesson: Isolated rep remarks aren't "commercial advertising." Organized, disseminated campaigns are. And standing follows proof of lost sales.
records.Notes:
- One of the first pharma cases applying the Supreme Court's Lexmark (2014) zone-of-interests and proximate cause standing test mid-litigation
- The court held a handful of one-off statements by individual reps didn't meet the "commercial advertising or promotion" element, which requires sufficient dissemination to the relevant market
- The dueling counterclaims make it a good teaching case: in a two-drug category, an advertising suit almost guarantees a mirror-image countersuit

records.Allegation Summary:
- GSK alleged BI's marketing brochures falsely claimed COPD patients can't inhale strongly enough for dry powder inhalers, making BI's soft mist Respimat superior
- GSK said the cited science didn't support the inhalation claim
records.Compliance Lesson: HCP-directed detailing claims need real scientific support behind every assertion. Courts will shut a campaign down fast in an $18B market.
records.Notes:
- The court dug into the actual pulmonology literature BI cited and found it didn't say what the brochure claimed, a reminder that reference-checking promo claims against the cited study is not a formality
- Injunction issued before GSK had to show market share loss, because likely falsity plus likely harm was enough
- Most instructive modern example of the Novartis substantiation framework applied to HCP-facing (not DTC) materials, which is exactly the material Medical Affairs reviews

records.Allegation Summary:
- Sandoz alleged Amgen ran a campaign claiming pegfilgrastim prefilled syringes are less effective and less safe than its Onpro on-body injector
- The campaign allegedly relied on studies FDA had already warned Amgen about using promotionally
records.Compliance Lesson: Disparaging biosimilars with weak data is a lawsuit magnet, doubly so if FDA already flagged the same studies.
records.Notes:
- The complaint leaned on the fact that FDA had previously objected to Amgen's use of the same data, letting Sandoz borrow regulatory credibility for its falsity argument
- Represents the biosimilar-era pattern: originators allegedly using safety/efficacy FUD to defend share against interchangeable competition
- Watch this space for Merz relevance: as neurotoxin competition grows, the same anti-disparagement logic applies to comparative claims between products in an established category

records.Allegation Summary: - Nexus alleged the competitor falsely claimed a drug shortage to steer customers to its own product
records.Compliance Lesson: The Lanham Act only reaches claims about a product's nature, characteristics, or qualities. Market-condition statements fall outside it.
records.Notes:
- The court held a shortage statement describes market availability, not the product itself, so it flunks the threshold "nature, characteristics, or qualities" element
- Useful precedent when assessing borderline claims: statements about supply, price, or corporate facts usually aren't Lanham material even if false
- Doesn't mean such statements are risk-free; they can still draw FTC, state consumer protection, or common law unfair competition exposure

records.Allegation Summary:
- Novo alleges false advertising for claims like "compounded semaglutide achieves the same results as Ozempic" and false statements that semaglutide products are "FDA-approved"
- Also trademark claims over use of the Ozempic and Wegovy marks in compounder marketing
records.Compliance Lesson: "Same as [approved drug]" equivalence claims are false advertising unless the product actually went through FDA. Trademark use compounds the exposure.
records.Notes:
- The largest coordinated pharma advertising enforcement campaign ever run by a single company, over 130 suits across 40 states
- Most defendants folded quickly into consent judgments restricting their claims (equivalence, FDA approval, safety) rather than banning sales outright
- Big-picture lesson: when FDA enforcement is slow (compounding during shortage), brand companies use the Lanham Act as a private enforcement substitute

records.Allegation Summary: - Lilly alleges Empower manufactured and sold untested weight loss drugs at commercial scale while misleading consumers about safety, efficacy, and regulatory status
records.Compliance Lesson: Safety and efficacy claims for unapproved products draw simultaneous fire from FDA and from the brand company whose market you're taking.
records.Notes:
- Filed after courts upheld FDA's removal of tirzepatide from the shortage list, which stripped compounders of their legal cover for mass production
- Lilly voluntarily dismissed its first New Jersey suit and refiled in Texas, a venue move worth noting for anyone tracking where these cases concentrate
- Alleges "personalized medicine" framing was a fig leaf for mass production, attacking the marketing angle compounders rely on

records.Allegation Summary:
- Lilly sued Strive for advertising mass-compounded tirzepatide as "clinically proven" and "personalized"
- Strive countersued Lilly and Novo in Jan. 2026 alleging they coordinated to suppress compounded GLP-1 competition
records.Compliance Lesson: Aggressive advertising enforcement can boomerang into antitrust counterclaims. Legal strategy has to price in the blowback.
records.Notes:
- "Clinically proven" is the key phrase: compounded products by definition have no clinical trials of their own, so the claim borrows the brand's data without the brand's approval
- Strive's antitrust theory (brands coordinating litigation to exclude competitors) is a live test of how far the Novo/Lilly enforcement wave can go before it creates its own liability
- Track this one through 2026; the outcome shapes whether the mass-litigation playbook survives

records.Issue Summary: - Pfizer challenged Salonpas ad claims; Hisamitsu refused to participate in the NAD process entirely
records.Outcome: Referred to FTC; FTC declined to act
records.Rule Established: Refusing to participate in NAD triggers an FTC referral, but referral is not a guarantee of enforcement.
records.Notes:
- The FTC's public decision not to act after the referral was unusual and weakened the deterrent behind NAD's referral threat
- Still risky to ignore NAD: the referral itself is public, gets press coverage, and lands the company on regulators' radar
- Most advertisers participate precisely because a cooperative NAD outcome is quieter and cheaper than what follows a referral

records.Issue Summary: - Challenge alleged Trulance advertising communicated a misleading message about competing products
records.Outcome: Referred to regulators; advertiser declined to participate
records.Rule Established: NAD has jurisdiction over prescription drug advertising, not just OTC and consumer products.
records.Notes:
- Before this, many assumed Rx advertising disputes belonged only to FDA and the courts; this decision put Rx squarely in NAD's lane
- Opened the door to the Kisqali and Nemluvio challenges below
- For a company deciding where to attack a rival's claims, NAD is now a real third option next to an OPDP complaint and a Lanham suit

records.Products: Kisqali vs. Verzenio (HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer)
records.Issue Summary:
- Lilly challenged claims that patients live longer on Kisqali, including "only drug in class with consistently proven overall survival benefit across three phase III trials"
- NAD reviewed both HCP-facing and consumer-facing versions of the messaging
records.Rule Established: The same claim can be acceptable to a physician audience and misleading to consumers. Audience sophistication changes the analysis.
records.Notes:
- NAD found the HCP materials had a reasonable basis because doctors can contextualize cross-trial comparisons, but the consumer versions communicated an unsupported superiority message
- Directly parallels the DTC vs. HCP audience distinction your OPDP sheets already track in the Audience column
- First major Rx-vs-Rx NAD decision in years, and the one that signaled pharma companies were ready to use NAD against each other

records.Issue Summary:
- Sanofi challenged "itch relief as soon as 48 hours" claims
- NAD found the post hoc analyses methodologically acceptable but only about 6% of eczema patients and 13% of PN patients achieved 48-hour relief after netting out placebo
records.Outcome: Modify or discontinue; Galderma agreed to complyv
records.Rule Established: An "as soon as" claim works like an "up to" claim: an appreciable share of patients must actually hit the stated benefit, and disclosures must be clear and conspicuous.
records.Notes:
- The data itself survived review; the problem was the gap between a headline speed claim and the small fraction of patients who actually experienced it
- NAD also dinged the disclosures as too small and too far from the claim, echoing OPDP's position that fine print can't cure a misleading main message
- Sanofi is Dupixent's maker, so this was a direct competitive strike in the dermatology market dressed as a self-regulatory challenge

records.Summary:
- Brukinsa TV ad implied patients would "see their futures clearly" and that it beats ibrutinib on cardiovascular safety
- Neither claim is backed by substantial evidence, and distracting visuals undercut the safety info
records.Notes: - BeOne's second Brukinsa untitled letter in 2026 (after the 01/2026 one)

records.Summary:
- Oral Wegovy ad implied the pill form beats other injectable GLP-1s ("live lighter"), which isn't proven
- Also didn't show the audio and text risk info at the same time
records.Notes: - Suggested pill-form benefits with no head-to-head data to support it

records.Summary:
- A second Vyvgart Hytrulo ad showed patients golfing and driving like they're back to normal
- Overstated CIDP improvement and skipped key risk info like the 30-minute post-injection monitoring
records.Notes: - Second untitled letter to argenx the same day for a different commercial

records.Summary:
- A second Vyvgart Hytrulo ad showed patients golfing and driving like they're back to normal
- Overstated CIDP improvement and skipped key risk info like the 30-minute post-injection monitoring
records.Notes: - Second untitled letter to argenx the same day for a different commercial

records.Summary:
- DTC TV ad ("Places You Want to Be") said Farxiga reduces the risk of cardiovascular death in CKD or heart failure
- That's only one piece of the approved indication. Farxiga is approved on broader composite endpoints (eGFR decline, end-stage kidney disease, HF hospitalization, etc.)
- By narrowing it to just "cardiovascular death," the ad misrepresented what the drug is actually approved to do
records.Notes: - Cherry-picking one endpoint from a composite indication is misleading. Close-out 11/25/2025

records.Summary:
- DTC TV ad ran a romantic story arc (Lewis meets a woman at the grocery store) during the boxed-warning major statement
- The scene changes, music, and "will they end up together" hook pulled attention away from the serious-infection risk being read
- The efficacy SUPER (the hemoglobin comparison vs Soliris/Ultomiris) flew by at 433 words per minute with flashy competing graphics, too fast to actually read
- Boxed warning is for serious and life-threatening infections
records.Notes: - Entertaining visuals during the major statement undercut risk communication. Close-out issued

records.Summary:
-Ingrezza TV ad showed near complete resoluition of TD symptoms, overstating how much patients actually improve
-Claimed rapid improvement at 2 weeks using exploratory data, even though the main study was at 6 weeks and ealier resluts weren't reliable
-Presented "98% patient satisfation" in a way that made it seem broadly applicable, even though the data came from a selective/enriched group of patients
records.Notes: - Using exploratory endpoints and selective patient populations can make efficacy results look stronger and more generalizable than they actually are

records.Summary:
-Kesimpta TV ad claimed the treatment can be taken in “1 minute a month,” oversimplifying the actual administration process and required steps
 - Downplayed important details like the need for first-dose guidance from a healthcare provider, making the treatment seem easier and faster than it is
 - Used distracting visuals during the major statement that pulled attention away from the risk information
records.Notes: - Oversimplifying administration and using distracting visuals can make treatment seem easier and safer than it actually is while reducing understanding of risk information

records.Summary:
- Jardiance TV ad used fast-paced dancing and movie-themed visuals that distracted from the communication of benefits and limitations of use
- Competing visuals, music, and scene changes made it harder for viewers to understand key information about the drug’s effectiveness
- Risk information was hard to read due to font size, placement, and ongoing distractions on screen
records.Notes: - Distracting visuals and poor text readability can interfere with understanding of both benefit and risk information, creating a misleading overall impression

records.Summary:
-Jardiance TV ad used fast-paced dancing and softball-themed visuals that distracted from the communication of benefits and limitations of use
- Competing visuals, music, and frequent scene changes made it harder for viewers to understand key effectiveness information
- Risk information was difficult to read due to poor font size, placement, and ongoing on-screen distraction
records.Notes: - Distractions and poor readability during the major statement can reduce understanding of both risk and benefit information, leading to a misleading overall impression

records.Summary:
-- Jardiance TV ad used fast-paced dancing and supermarket-themed visuals that distracted from the communication of benefits and limitations of use
- Competing visuals, music, and frequent scene changes made it harder for viewers to understand key effectiveness information
- Risk information was difficult to read due to poor font size, placement, and ongoing on-screen distractions
records.Notes: - Distracting visuals and poor text readability during the major statement can interfere with understanding of risk and benefit information, creating a misleading impression

records.Summary:
- Jublia TV ad showed a patient’s nail fungus completely resolving after treatment, implying a guaranteed or complete cure
 - Used messaging like “don’t fear the fungus” that made the condition seem easy to treat and minimized the actual variability in outcomes
 - Suggested near-immediate results, even though treatment requires long-term use (up to 48 weeks) and cure rates are relatively low
records.Notes: - Ads that show fast or complete resolution can overpromise outcomes when real-world cure rates are limited and require long treatment durations

records.Summary:
- Kisqali TV ad claimed the drug helps patients live longer using OS (Overall Survival) data from an exploratory analysis, making the benefit seem more certain than it is
 - Presented survival data without proper context, even though the exploratory analysis was not pre-specified and may be unreliable
 - Included key efficacy information in a fast-moving SUPER (on-screen text) that was too dense and fast for viewers to read and understand
records.Notes: - Using exploratory survival data without clear context and presenting it too quickly can mislead viewers about the true strength of evidence

records.Summary:
- Linzess TV ad used frequent scene changes and upbeat family visuals during the major statement that distracted from risk information
 - Emotional and engaging visuals competed for attention while safety information was being presented 
- Overall presentation made it harder for viewers to hear, read, and understand the major statement
records.Notes: - Attention-grabbing or emotional visuals during the major statement can reduce comprehension of risk information and create a misleading impression

records.Summary:
- Neffy TV ad claimed patients can say “goodbye to needles,” suggesting they would no longer need injectable treatments for allergic emergencies
 - Implied the product eliminates needle fear and emotional concerns, despite no evidence supporting this type of benefit 
- Suggested patients could freely engage in activities (like eating risky foods) without worry, overstating the level of protection the drug provides
records.Notes: - Ads that imply reduced need for caution or complete freedom from risk can mislead patients about appropriate safety behaviors, especially for serious conditions like anaphylaxis

records.Violation Category: Misleading efficacy / misleading representation of evidence
records.Summary:
- Nemluvio TV ad suggested the drug can be used alone, failing to clearly communicate it must be used with topical treatments
 - Claimed rapid itch relief within 48 hours using post-hoc (after-the-fact) data that wasn’t pre-specified or statistically reliable
 - Suggested many patients achieve and maintain clear skin, overstating results when actual response rates were limited
records.Notes: - Using post-hoc data and not clearly stating combination use can make efficacy look stronger and more broadly applicable than supported by the evidence

records.Summary:
- Opdivo Qvantig TV ad emphasized fast 3-minute treatment, suggesting patients spend minimal time in clinical settings 
- Visuals showed patients enjoying activities instead of being in treatment, overstating convenience and real-world experience
 - Frequent scene changes and engaging visuals during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.Notes: - Ads that overemphasize convenience and lifestyle benefits can mislead patients about the actual treatment experience and time required

records.Summary:
- Opdivo + Yervoy TV ad showed patients living active, unrestricted lives, suggesting they can maintain normal functioning during treatment
 - Implied improvements in HRQoL, including emotional and social well-being, without supporting evidence
 - Attention-grabbing visuals and frequent scene changes during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.Notes: - Ads that portray patients as fully functional or improved in QoL without evidence can mislead expectations about real treatment experience

records.Summary:
- Qelbree TV ad showed complete resolution of ADHD symptoms, overstating the level of improvement seen in clinical studies
 - Portrayed patients as fully symptom-free, even though actual score improvements were more modest
 - Used frequent scene changes and positive visuals during the major statement, which distracted from and minimized the seriousness of risks
records.Notes: - Ads that show full symptom resolution and pair risk info with upbeat visuals can overstate efficacy and downplay safety concerns

records.Summary:
- Qelbree TV ad showed complete resolution of ADHD symptoms, overstating the level of improvement seen in clinical studies
- Portrayed patients as fully functional and symptom-free, even though actual improvements were more modest
- Used frequent scene changes and upbeat visuals during the major statement, distracting from and minimizing the communication of risks
records.Notes: - Showing full symptom resolution and pairing risk info with positive visuals can overstate effectiveness and downplay safety concerns

records.Summary:
- Qulipta TV ad used a before-and-after storyline that suggested complete prevention of migraines, overstating the drug’s actual effect
- Messaging like “more zero-migraine days” and “forget-you-get migraine medicine” implied patients would stop having migraines entirely
- Attention-grabbing visuals and scene changes during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.Notes: - Ads that imply total symptom elimination (like zero migraines) can mislead when clinical data only shows modest reductions in migraine days

records.Summary:
- Pluvicto TV ad used storytelling and success visuals that suggested the drug would lead to positive outcomes despite prior treatment failures 
- Omitted key parts of the approved indication, making it seem appropriate for a broader patient population than actually approved
 - Used frequent scene changes and engaging visuals during the major statement, distracting from the communication of risk information
records.Notes: - Omitting key indication limits and implying guaranteed success can make a treatment seem more broadly effective than supported by clinical data

records.Summary:
- Truqap TV ad used frequent scene changes and visually engaging transitions during the major statement that distracted from risk information 
- Creative visuals and storytelling elements competed for viewer attention while safety information was being presented
- Overall presentation made it harder for viewers to process and understand the major statement
records.Notes: - Complex visuals and frequent transitions during the major statement can interfere with comprehension of important safety information

records.Summary:
- Tzield TV ad suggested the drug can improve QoL and emotional well-being beyond delaying disease progression
- Messaging implied patients could “win” against type 1 diabetes, overstating the drug’s actual benefit
- Frequent scene changes and attention-grabbing visuals during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.Notes: - Claims implying improved QoL or “winning” a disease can mislead when the drug is only shown to delay progression, not improve overall functioning

records.Summary:
- Velsipity TV ad used a visual “creature” to represent ulcerative colitis, showing a dramatic transformation that overstated treatment effectiveness 
- Depicted near-complete control of symptoms, even though clinical remission rates were relatively limited
 - The same visuals during the major statement distracted from and interfered with the communication of risk information
records.Notes: - Exaggerated visual metaphors can make modest clinical improvements appear like complete symptom control, misleading patients about expected results

records.Summary:
- Ultomiris TV ad claimed “continuous symptom control” and strong improvements in daily activities, overstating the drug’s actual demonstrated benefit
 - Presented outcome measures in a way that implied broader and consistent control than supported by the data 
- Frequent scene changes and engaging visuals during the major statement distracted from the communication of risk information
records.Notes: - Framing limited clinical endpoints (like MG-ADL – Myasthenia Gravis Activities of Daily Living) as continuous or complete control can overstate real-world effectiveness

records.Summary:
- Claimed that one dose “quickly eliminates migraine pain,” overstating both the speed and extent of benefit compared to clinical data
- Used strong before-and-after visuals suggesting rapid and complete symptom relief, which is not supported by study results
- Implied that most or all patients achieve pain elimination after one dose, even though only a minority of patients achieved pain freedom in trials
records.Notes: - Claims of rapid or complete symptom elimination must match clinical trial endpoints; using strong visuals and celebrity endorsements can amplify misleading efficacy impressions

records.Summary:
- Claimed Kisqali “preserves quality of life” and patients are “living well,” based on exploratory PRO data that cannot support definitive conclusions
- Suggested survival benefits (“live longer,” “long live life”) in a way that overstated or miscommunicated the actual clinical data
- Presented important qualifying information in a fast, hard-to-read SUPER with distracting visuals and audio, reducing viewers’ ability to understand the true efficacy results
records.Notes: - Exploratory or secondary endpoints (like QoL PROs) cannot support strong claims, and key data in SUPERs must be readable and not undermined by distracting visuals

records.Summary: - Claimed Rexulti provides a “62% greater reduction” in depression symptoms, which significantly overstates the actual treatment effect - Misrepresented clinical data by using a percentage that exaggerates the real improvement compared to baseline and placebo differences - Implied patients would experience a large reduction in symptoms, when actual data showed only modest improvement
records.Notes: - Misusing percentages or statistics to inflate treatment effects is misleading, especially when actual clinical improvements are much smaller

records.Violation Category: Omission of risk information / failure to submit
records.Summary:
- Two TV commercials for Emgality made promotional claims but left out all the risk info
- No major side effects or safety warnings shown at all
records.Notes: - Ads were never submitted to FDA on Form 2253 like they're supposed to be

records.Summary:
- Omitted important risk information, including hypersensitivity reactions and serious consequences of contraindications
- Claimed the product is “easy to use” and users would know they “did it right,” overstating ease and confidence without supporting evidence
- Failed to clearly communicate proper use context, suggesting it can be used in mild situations rather than severe hypoglycemia requiring caregiver assistance
records.Notes: - Ease-of-use claims must reflect actual administration complexity, especially for emergency-use products, and proper use context must be clearly explained

records.Violation Category: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.Summary:
- Omitted key risk information, including important contraindications and serious risks like pelvic infection and device expulsion
- Presented risk information in a way that was difficult to understand due to fast-paced visuals, music, and competing distractions
- Repeated “hormone-free” claims that minimized perceived risk and suggested improved safety compared to other contraceptives
records.Notes: - Even accurate claims (e.g., “hormone-free”) can be misleading if they imply improved safety or distract from serious risks

records.Violation Category: Omission of risk information / misleading presentation
records.Summary:
- Included limited risk statements but omitted other serious contraindications and boxed warning details, creating a misleading impression of safety
- Failed to adequately communicate neuropsychiatric risks and other serious safety concerns
- Presented risk information in SUPERs only or alongside competing audio messages, reducing clarity and comprehension
records.Notes: - Partial risk disclosure is not sufficient; missing key risks or presenting them unclearly can still mislead about overall safety

records.Summary:
- Nubeqa YouTube video and its Spanish TV version left out critical risk info for prostate cancer use
- Skipped detailed warnings on heart disease and seizures, so safety looked better than it is
records.Notes: - Also botched the dual audio/text risk presentation and used fast visuals that distracted from safety

records.Summary:
- Podcast and video for Anktiva overstated efficacy ("cure" and stopping recurrence) for bladder cancer
- Suggested it helps with other cancers beyond the approved use, with no real safety info
records.Notes: - Second untitled letter to ImmunityBio (after 09/2025); they kept going and got a warning letter in March 2026

records.Summary:
- Voquezna interview video featured a spokesperson discussing the drug’s benefits and personal experience
 - Encouraged use and directed viewers to seek more information, but did not include any risk or safety information
 - Completely omitted risk disclosures, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
records.Notes: - Any promotional content that includes benefit claims must also include risk information, even in interviews or spokesperson-led content

records.Summary:
- Voquezna interview video featured a spokesperson discussing GERD and use of the drug
 - Included benefit-related messaging and personal experience claims without any safety or risk information
 - Completely omitted risk disclosures, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
records.Notes: - Even informal content like interviews must include risk information when benefit claims are mentioned

records.Violation Category: Omission of risk information / failure to submit
records.Summary:
- Voquezna interview video featured a spokesperson discussing personal experience and benefits of the drug 
- Included statements suggesting effectiveness and encouraging use, but directed viewers elsewhere for more information instead of presenting it directly 
- Completely omitted risk disclosures, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
records.Notes: - Pointing viewers to a website or doctor does not replace the need to include risk information alongside benefit claims

records.Summary:
- Claimed rapid relief within 15–30 minutes based on a spokesperson testimonial, despite no clinical data supporting efficacy at that timeframe 
- Suggested superiority over other treatments (e.g., “game changer,” no rebound headaches), without supporting comparative evidence 
- Minimized risk information by presenting it briefly, in small text at the end of the video, and failed to clearly communicate indication limitations
records.Notes: - Celebrity testimonials cannot support clinical claims, and personal experiences cannot be used to imply rapid onset or superiority without evidence

records.Summary:
- Omitted serious risks such as potential eye injury and severe skin reactions, minimizing the drug’s safety concerns
- Suggested typical outcomes of complete lesion clearance using before-and-after images, overstating effectiveness compared to clinical data
- Presented limited side effect information (e.g., stinging only) while failing to disclose the full range and duration of common adverse reactions
records.Notes: - Before-and-after visuals and testimonials cannot be used to imply typical outcomes or downplay risks without corresponding clinical evidence

records.Summary:
- Used patient testimonials to suggest reduced or no side effects compared to other statins, implying improved safety without supporting evidence
- Created a misleading impression of superiority by showing patients switching from other statins and experiencing fewer side effects
- Minimized risk communication by presenting safety information as scrolling text that was difficult to read and overshadowed by testimonial content
records.Notes: - Testimonials cannot be used to imply improved tolerability or safety without evidence, and risk information must be presented clearly and prominently

records.Violation Category: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.Summary:
- Completely omitted all risk information, despite the drug having boxed warnings for serious conditions (e.g., cancer and cardiovascular risks)
- Used patient testimony suggesting no side effects, reinforcing a misleading impression of safety
- Claimed “instant relief” of symptoms, which is not supported by clinical evidence
records.Notes: - Omission of all risk information, especially for drugs with boxed warnings, is a major violation, and patient testimonials cannot replace required safety disclosures

records.Summary:
- Turalio patient video used dramatized before-and-after scenes implying everyone with TGCT fully recovers
- Real numbers are way lower (around 38% response, only 15% complete response)
records.Notes: - Extra misleading given Turalio's boxed warning for liver toxicity, which the video didn't convey

records.Summary:
- Omitted important safety information in the video, including serious risks like embryofetal risk and photosensitivity, while presenting strong benefit claims
- Suggested product superiority and unique status (“first and only”) and implied effectiveness in patients who failed other treatments, without supporting evidence
- Used post hoc analyses and manipulated data (e.g., removing vehicle effect) to claim “synergy” and greater efficacy, overstating the drug’s actual benefit
records.Notes: - Post hoc analyses and selective data presentation cannot support strong claims, and risk information must be clearly presented alongside benefit claims

records.Summary:
- Used patient testimonials to suggest reduced or no side effects compared to other statins, implying improved safety without supporting evidence
- Created a misleading impression of superiority by showing patients switching from other statins due to side effects and improving on Livalo
- Minimized risk communication by presenting safety information as scrolling text that was difficult to read and overshadowed by testimonials
records.Notes: - Patient testimonials cannot be used to imply reduced side effects or superiority without evidence, and risk information must be presented clearly and prominently

- Minimized risk communication by presenting safety information as scrolling text that was difficult to read and overshadowed by testimonial content
records.Notes: - Testimonials cannot be used to imply improved tolerability or safety without evidence, and risk information must be presented clearly and prominently

records.Summary:
- Adcetris slide deck had an observational all-cause mortality claim and graphs overstating survival and progression benefit
- Small disclaimer included, but FDA said it doesn't fix the misleading impression
records.Notes: - A disclaimer was there but didn't actually correct the misleading claims

records.Summary:
- Fasenra consumer promos talked up asthma symptom improvement with no required risk info
- Skipped serious reactions like anaphylaxis
records.Notes: - Two separate Fasenra materials (like online ads) got cited together in one letter

- Skipped serious reactions like anaphylaxis
