records.Summary:
- Fasenra consumer promos talked up asthma symptom improvement with no required risk info
- Skipped serious reactions like anaphylaxis
records.Notes: - Two separate Fasenra materials (like online ads) got cited together in one letter

records.Summary:
- Presented benefits prominently in an influencer-style video while risk information was delayed, hard to read, and easy to miss, minimizing serious safety risks
 - Suggested rapid or same-day treatment effects (getting ready for a “big night out”), which is not supported by clinical data
 - Implied product superiority (“smart tox” / manufacturing advantage) without evidence comparing it to other botulinum toxin products
records.Notes: Social media and influencer content must present risk information clearly and alongside benefits; fast, hidden, or delayed risk disclosures and unsupported superiority claims are misleading

records.Summary:
- Paid a celebrity to promote Auvi-Q on Instagram to parents of kids with allergies
- Post only talked benefits and had zero risk info
records.Notes: - Dropped a link for safety info instead of actually saying the risks in the post

records.Summary:
- Presented benefit claims but omitted all risk information, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety
 - Claimed patients would have “periods on a schedule,” overstating predictability of bleeding patterns not supported by clinical data
- Ignored evidence of irregular and unscheduled bleeding, which is common with the drug and contradicts the claim
records.Notes: - Social media posts must include risk information alongside benefits, and claims about predictable outcomes must reflect real clinical variability

records.Summary: - Highlighted benefit claim (A1C reduction) but failed to adequately communicate the full indication and key limitations of use - Presented risk information in small, fast-scrolling text that was difficult to read and overshadowed by distracting visuals and audio - Omitted important risk details (e.g., hypoglycemia risk with other medications), creating a misleading impression about safety
records.Notes: - Risk and indication information must be clearly presented and readable; fast-paced or hidden disclosures do not fix misleading impressions

records.Summary:
- Alymsys (a bevacizumab biosimilar) ad copied the reference product's efficacy claims
- Didn't properly present the safety info like hemorrhage and GI perforation risk
records.Notes: - Biosimilars have to follow the same promo and risk-disclosure rules as the reference biologic

records.Summary:
- Ozempic digital ad pushed glucose and weight benefits for weight loss
- Didn't include the major risk info like the thyroid C-cell tumor risk
records.Notes: - DTC online marketing still has to carry the major risk disclosures

records.Summary: - Claimed Rexulti provides a “62% greater reduction” in depression symptoms, which significantly overstates the actual treatment effect - Misrepresented clinical data by using a percentage that exaggerates the real improvement compared to baseline and placebo differences - Implied patients would experience a large reduction in symptoms, when actual data showed only modest improvement
records.Notes: - Misusing percentages or statistics to inflate treatment effects is misleading, especially when actual clinical improvements are much smaller

records.Summary:
- Presented benefit and use claims in sponsored search links without including any risk information
- Highlighted dosing flexibility and pediatric convenience without communicating serious safety risks
- Created a misleading impression of safety by omitting risk information for a drug used in a vulnerable pediatric population
records.Notes: - Even short formats like search ads must include risk information when making benefit claims, especially for drugs with serious risks

records.Summary:
- Claimed higher risk of febrile neutropenia with prefilled syringe vs Onpro device based on a real-world study with significant design limitations 
- Used an unvalidated algorithm and uncontrolled patient differences, making the comparative results unreliable 
- Presented relative risk differences prominently (31%) despite very small absolute differences and potential bias, overstating clinical significance
records.Notes: - Comparative claims require well-controlled evidence; flawed real-world studies with bias and small absolute differences cannot support superiority claims

records.Summary:
- Amvuttra materials again overstated polyneuropathy improvement beyond what was shown
- Didn't communicate the monitoring need and injection site reactions, repeating the earlier problem
records.Notes: - Second Amvuttra untitled letter since Sept 2025, so repeated compliance issues

records.Summary:
- Tremfya materials pushed joint relief and skin clearance for psoriatic arthritis
- Left out required risk info for a systemic immunosuppressant (infection, malignancy) and overstated efficacy
records.Notes: - Similar issues had come up before in OPDP letters for other psoriasis biologics

records.Summary:
- Vonjo brochure showed spleen and symptom benefits for myelofibrosis
- Didn't communicate the safety risks well, mainly blood and heart toxicities
records.Notes: - Close-out letter issued after Sobi fixed the concerns

records.Summary:
- Brukinsa presentation made unsubstantiated survival claims for B-cell cancers
- Implied a better safety profile than ibrutinib that wasn't actually proven
records.Notes: - Close-out letter came in 02/2026 after fixes, but a later DTC campaign got them another letter

records.Summary:
- Nexlizet sales aid hyped lipid-lowering and cardiovascular benefits
- Played down needing a statin and glossed over risks like hyperuricemia and tendon rupture
records.Notes: - A two-ingredient combo product needs careful balance of safety vs efficacy

records.Summary:
- Xcopri piece covered its epilepsy efficacy but left out important safety info
- Skipped warnings about serious skin reactions
records.Notes: - Risk of serious rashes and DRESS wasn't fully addressed

records.Summary:
- Apretude slide deck made HIV prevention efficacy claims but skipped the risk and safety info
- No balance of risks vs benefits at all
records.Notes: - Straight up no risk disclosures, which breaks fair balance

records.Summary:
- Iclusig brochure hyped the leukemia benefits but didn't properly lay out the serious risks
- Skipped stuff like arterial occlusion and thrombosis
records.Notes: - Iclusig has a boxed warning, and the risk disclosure wasn't enough

records.Summary:
- Jeuveau materials pushed the cosmetic wrinkle benefits with no required risk info
- Left out things like the toxin potentially spreading and other serious effects
records.Notes: - First OPDP untitled letter for an aesthetic/cosmetic product

records.Summary:
- Pemazyre piece claimed benefits in cholangiocarcinoma without solid evidence
- Played down the safety issues, so the efficacy and tolerability looked better than they are
records.Notes: - Claims went past the limited clinical evidence they actually had

records.Summary:
- Photofrin material made treatment claims for photodynamic therapy
- Either left out or barely mentioned the major risks and contraindications
records.Notes: - Serious safety stuff like photosensitivity wasn't disclosed well

records.Notes: - Serious safety stuff like photosensitivity wasn't disclosed well

records.Summary:
- Rystiggo content implied better efficacy or patient outcomes than the data supports for myasthenia gravis
- Could mislead providers on what the drug actually does
records.Notes: - First OPDP untitled letter for Rystiggo

records.Summary:
- Skyrizi presentation pushed the plaque psoriasis efficacy but left out important risk info from the label
- Skipped things like infection risk
records.Notes: - Risk info straight from Skyrizi's label got omitted, breaking fair balance

records.Summary:
- Tazverik content made broad benefit claims for epithelioid sarcoma without solid evidence
- Downplayed the safety monitoring you actually need, like secondary cancer risk
records.Notes: - OPDP's first enforcement on a Tazverik promo piece

records.Summary:
- Austedo XR communications overstated how much patients improve with tardive dyskinesia
- Minimized known risks like depression and suicidality
records.Notes: - Those serious warnings weren't described well enough

- Minimized known risks like depression and suicidality
records.Notes: - Those serious warnings weren't described well enough

records.Summary:
- Auvelity material talked up fast benefits and remission rates that overstate how well it works for depression
- Didn't disclose the suicidality warning and other risks
records.Notes: - Auvelity's boxed warning for suicidal thoughts in young adults wasn't conveyed right

records.Summary:
- Bimzelx visual aids had unbalanced efficacy info (like high clearance rates) for plaque psoriasis
- No proper risk disclosure on infection risk and lab abnormalities
records.Notes: - Materials implied superiority and skipped a full look at the safety profile

records.Summary:
- Calquence materials implied it beats other BTK inhibitors for CLL, like better survival
- Used indirect comparisons that aren't head-to-head and aren't backed by solid evidence
records.Notes: - Comparative claims had no direct comparison trial data behind them

records.Summary:
- Camzyos communications hyped symptom and outcome improvements for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy in broad terms
- Didn't properly mention the required REMS or the heart failure risk
records.Notes: - Camzyos's boxed warning about heart failure wasn't disclosed prominently

records.Summary:
- Contrave consumer brochure overstated weight loss results and suggested broad use
- Left out risks like neuropsychiatric events and blood pressure increases
records.Notes: - Weight-loss drugs with real safety concerns need balanced messaging

records.Violation Category: Misleading safety comparisons / risk minimization / unsupported comparative claims
records.Summary:
- Nextstellis speaker deck suggested the drug is safer than other CHCs  using unsupported “low-impact” estrogen claims 
- Used comparative charts and statistical references (like p-values) to imply safety advantages that were not backed by evidence 
- Minimized and omitted serious risks (e.g., liver effects, thromboembolic risk, breast cancer risk) and misrepresented adverse event data
records.Notes: - Comparative safety claims require strong evidence; selective data and misused statistics can mislead HCP decision-making

records.Summary:
- Claimed Phexxi prevents pregnancy 99% of the time using a “per act of sex” analysis, which overstates effectiveness 
- Used an invalid methodology that ignores variation in pregnancy likelihood across the menstrual cycle (fertile window)
 - Misrepresented efficacy compared to approved labeling, which shows lower real
world pregnancy prevention rates
records.Notes: - Using non-standard or unsupported efficacy metrics (like per-act analysis) can inflate results and mislead about real-world effectiveness

records.Summary:
- Claimed large LDL-C reductions by combining results from separate studies, using a scientifically unsupported cross-study calculation 
- Suggested specific treatment effects and target outcomes (e.g., LDL-C <70 mg/dL) that were not reliably supported by clinical trial design or endpoints 
- Minimized risk information by presenting benefits prominently while relegating serious risks to less visible, harder-to-read sections
records.Notes: - Combining results across different studies to create efficacy claims is not valid, and risk information must be presented with equal prominence to benefits

records.Summary:
- Claimed EstroGel provides the “lowest effective dose” of estrogen, implying superiority over other estrogen products
- Suggested no other therapies offer lower effective doses, which is not supported by available FDA-approved products
- Relied on studies that did not compare EstroGel to other estrogen therapies, making the comparative claim unsupported
records.Notes: - Claims of “lowest dose” or similar superiority require direct comparative evidence; indirect or unrelated studies cannot support such claims

records.Summary:
- Promoted an investigational imaging drug as safe and effective even though it was not FDA-approved
- Made strong claims about accuracy, superiority, and patient benefit without established evidence
- Suggested no significant risks and improved outcomes, which are not allowed for unapproved products
records.Notes: - Investigational drugs cannot be promoted as safe or effective before FDA approval; even strong preliminary claims are considered improper promotion

records.Summary:
- Promotional emails for Sucraid left out material risk info from the label
- Didn't mention severe hypersensitivity reactions (Sucraid contains papain), raised blood glucose in diabetics, or the dietary starch restriction note
- Also listed the risks out of severity order (adverse reactions before the more serious precautions), which downplays them
- Graphics/colors in the emails weren't enough to fix the misleading impression
records.Notes: - Risk info has to be complete and ordered by severity, even in a simple email

records.Violation Category: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.Summary:
- Presented benefit claims prominently while omitting important warnings and precautions beyond the boxed warning 
- Failed to include key safety risk regarding improper dosing, which can lead to fatal toxicity 
- Did not adequately communicate the full indication, omitting that the drug is for pediatric patients and part of combination therapy
records.Notes: - Including only a boxed warning is not sufficient; all material risks must be included, and indications must be fully and clearly stated

records.Violation Category: Omission of risk information / misleading efficacy
records.Summary:
- Presented benefit and convenience claims while omitting all risk information, creating a misleading impression about the drug’s safety despite multiple known serious risks
- Highlighted reduced tablet burden and bioequivalence without providing any corresponding safety context
- Used a flawed real-world study to suggest improved adherence versus generic dexamethasone, despite major design issues that make the comparison unreliable
records.Notes: =D85- Omitting all risk information alongside benefit claims is a major violation; weak or biased study design cannot support comparative adherence or efficacy claims

records.Summary:
- Promoted Fycompa for unapproved uses, suggesting safety and effectiveness beyond its FDA-approved indication
- Provided statements indicating use in populations or settings not yet approved at the time
- Minimized serious boxed warning risks (psychiatric and behavioral effects), downplaying concerns and discouraging caution
records.Notes: - Sales representatives cannot promote off-label uses or downplay boxed warnings; verbal promotion is held to the same standards as formal materials

records.Summary:
- Promoted an investigational drug as effective for treating AML, including claims of mutation eradication and combination use with chemotherapy
- Suggested clinical benefit and superiority versus other therapies without established evidence
- Failed to clearly disclose that the product is investigational and not FDA-approved, creating a misleading impression of established use
records.Notes: - Investigational drugs cannot be promoted as safe, effective, or superior, especially in venues like conferences where they may appear alongside approved products

records.Summary:
- Emphasized abuse-deterrent properties while failing to adequately communicate serious risks, including addiction, abuse, overdose, and death
- Omitted key limitations of use, particularly that the drug should only be used when alternative treatments are inadequate
- Presented qualifying information about limitations of abuse-deterrent properties in a way that was not visible or prominent, undermining risk communication
records.Notes: - Highlighting abuse-deterrent features can be misleading if it implies the drug is safer; opioids still carry significant risks regardless of formulation

records.Summary:
- neffy (epinephrine nasal spray) statements talked it up as an epinephrine alternative before approval
- Made it sound available and beneficial when it wasn't approved yet
records.Notes: - Promoting an investigational anaphylaxis treatment as a ready option got OPDP's attention

records.Summary:
- Tevimbra slides suggested broad anti-cancer benefit before FDA approval
- No proper context or approved labeling behind it
records.Notes: - Unauthorized promotion of an immunotherapy that wasn't approved yet

records.Summary:
- Pushed KarXT (Cobenfy) before it was approved, suggesting it works for schizophrenia
- Product was still under FDA review at the time
records.Notes: - OPDP called it impermissible pre-approval promotion of an unapproved drug

records.Summary:
- Ketamine press release made therapeutic claims for mental health uses beyond what's approved
- Promoted an unapproved use with no evidence, and no risk talk for a Schedule II controlled substance
records.Notes: - Promoting an ANDA ketamine product for unapproved indications triggered OPDP

records.Summary:
- FYARRO presentation made broad efficacy claims for a rare cancer and downplayed the risks
- Made it sound way more effective than the safety context supports
records.Notes: - Probably the first enforcement action on FYARRO promo materials

records.Summary:
- Promoted Anktiva while it was still investigational, claiming it cures multiple cancers and stops recurrence
- CEO pushed unproven survival rates and called it safe and uniquely effective
records.Notes:
- Curing-cancer claims and use way beyond the approved bladder cancer indication weren't backed up
- First untitled letter to ImmunityBio in this wave

glossary.Definition: Federal criminal law (42 U.S.C. 1320a-7b) banning payment of anything of value to induce referrals or purchases of items reimbursed by federal healthcare programs. Speaker fees, copay support, and lavish meals become kickbacks when used as inducements.

A published drug price benchmark once used by Medicare and Medicaid for reimbursement. Companies that inflated AWP to widen the spread between reimbursement and actual cost got hit with fraud settlements.
A biologic approved as highly similar to an already-approved reference biologic, with no clinically meaningful differences. Marketing fights over biosimilar equivalence drive modern Lanham Act suits.
glossary.Where It Appears: OPDP Untitled Letters, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, Banner & Display Ads

glossary.Definition: A formal request asking FDA to take or withhold regulatory action. The proper channel when a dispute is really about a product's regulatory status, as opposed to suing under the Lanham Act.

The standard a qualifying disclosure has to meet: big enough, close enough to the claim, and readable enough to actually change the message a consumer takes away. Fine print doesn't cure a misleading headline.

Threshold element of a Lanham Act claim. The statement must be commercial speech, made to influence purchases, and disseminated widely enough to the relevant market. A few one-off sales rep remarks don't qualify.
Any promotional claim measuring one product against a competitor (stronger, faster, superior). Must be supported by head-to-head evidence matching the specific comparison made.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Untitled Letters, Direct Quotes, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, TV Commercials, Banner & Display Ads, Sales Aids & Print

glossary.Definition: A drug mixed or altered by a pharmacy for an individual patient, exempt from FDA approval under limited conditions. Mass-produced compounded GLP-1s marketed as equivalent to approved drugs triggered the 2023-2026 litigation wave.
A settlement entered as a court order. Violating it is contempt of court, not just breach of contract. How most Novo Nordisk compounding suits ended.

A multi-year compliance agreement with HHS-OIG that usually rides along with a DOJ settlement: mandatory monitoring, training, audits, and reporting in exchange for not being excluded from federal programs.
Face-to-face promotion of a drug to healthcare providers by sales reps. Oral detailing statements are legally actionable advertising (Schering v. Pfizer).
Promotion aimed at patients and the general public rather than clinicians. Held to a stricter standard because consumers can't contextualize clinical data the way physicians can.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, Direct Quotes, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, NAD Challenges, TV Commercials, YouTube, Podcasts & Interviews, Online Video, Social Media, Banner & Display Ads, Sales Aids & Print

glossary.Definition: A claim stating or implying scientific proof ("clinically proven," "studies show"). Plaintiff only needs to show the cited tests don't prove the claim, an easier path than proving outright falsity.
FDA's requirement that promotional material present risk information comparably to benefit information in prominence and readability. Risk omission is its violation.
glossary.Where It Appears: OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, Sales Aids & Print

glossary.Definition: A claim that never states the false message outright but unavoidably communicates it. Courts treat it like literal falsity, no consumer survey needed (Novartis v. J&J-Merck).

Federal law (31 U.S.C. 3729) imposing treble damages for knowingly causing false claims to be submitted to the government. The engine behind nearly every DOJ pharma settlement: off-label promotion and kickbacks cause pharmacies and providers to bill federal programs falsely.
glossary.Where It Appears: DOJ Settlements, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, Promo Risk Map

glossary.Definition: The Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, the statute FDA enforces. Only the government can enforce it; competitors can't use the Lanham Act as a backdoor to litigate FDCA violations (Sandoz v. Richardson-Vicks).
glossary.Where It Appears: DOJ Settlements, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits

glossary.Definition: The form manufacturers must use to submit promotional materials to FDA at the time of first use. "Failure to submit (2253)" is one of the violation types tracked in this workbook.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, TV Commercials

glossary.Definition: Physicians, NPs, PAs, pharmacists: the professional audience for promotion. HCP-directed claims get more latitude than DTC because of audience sophistication, but still require substantiation.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, Direct Quotes, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, NAD Challenges, Online Video, Social Media, Banner & Display Ads, Sales Aids & Print, Email, Conferences & In-Person

glossary.Definition: Anything of value given to induce prescribing or purchasing: sham speaker program fees, copay foundation funding, specialty pharmacy payments. The single most common theme in the DOJ Settlements sheet.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, DOJ Settlements, Promo Risk Map, Penalty v revenue

glossary.Definition: 15 U.S.C. 1125(a), the federal false advertising statute. Lets one company sue another over false or misleading statements of fact in commercial advertising that cause competitive injury. The basis for nearly every case on the Pharma v Pharma sheet.

Supreme Court test (Lexmark v. Static Control, 2014) for who can sue under the Lanham Act: the plaintiff's injury must fall in the statute's zone of interests (commercial reputation or lost sales) and be proximately caused by the false advertising.

The two ways an ad can be false. Literally false claims are enjoined without audience evidence. Impliedly false claims are literally true but misleading, and require extrinsic evidence (usually consumer surveys) proving the audience received the false message.
Compressing right-skewed data by taking natural logarithms so a few giant values (like Purdue's $5.5B penalty) don't dominate the analysis. Used in the ln(Rev) vs ln(Penalty) test.
An FDCA violation where a drug's labeling (which includes promotion) is false or misleading, or lacks adequate directions for use. The criminal hook in most off-label DOJ cases.

The ad industry's self-regulatory forum, run by BBB National Programs. Competitors challenge each other's claims there; NAD can only recommend changes and refer non-compliance to FTC or FDA. Faster and cheaper than a lawsuit, and it covers prescription drugs.

Promoting a drug for a use, dose, or population outside its approved labeling. Draws OPDP letters, DOJ/FCA settlements, and competitor Lanham suits (Zeneca v. Lilly). Physicians may prescribe off-label; companies may not promote it.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, DOJ Settlements, OPDP Untitled Letters, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, Conferences & In-Person, Promo Risk Map

glossary.Definition: FDA's Office of Prescription Drug Promotion, which reviews drug advertising and issues Warning and Untitled Letters. The source of the enforcement letters tracked in this workbook.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, NAD Challenges, TV Commercials, YouTube, Podcasts & Interviews, Online Video, Social Media, Banner & Display Ads, Sales Aids & Print, Email, Conferences & In-Person, Investor & Press, Promo Risk Map, Company Profile

glossary.Definition: A data point far outside the rest of the distribution. Purdue's $5.5B penalty is the outlier that wrecks the raw Pearson correlation on the Penalty Analysis sheet.

The probability of seeing a result at least this extreme if there were actually no relationship. Below 0.05 is the conventional threshold for statistical significance used in the Penalty Analysis tests.
glossary.Where It Appears: OPDP Untitled Letters, Sales Aids & Print, Penalty Analysis

glossary.Definition: Measures the strength of a straight-line relationship between two variables using their actual values. Sensitive to outliers and skew, which is why the raw-dollar version on the Penalty Analysis sheet shows nothing.
Treated as false without further proof. Under Novartis, a claim made with no substantiation whatsoever can be per se false, flipping the practical burden onto the advertiser.
Analysis run on trial data after the fact, looking at endpoints the trial wasn't designed to test. Acceptable support only with caveats; the Nemluvio 48-hour claims rested on post hoc analyses.
glossary.Where It Appears: OPDP Untitled Letters, NAD Challenges, Online Video

glossary.Definition: Promoting a drug (or a new use) before FDA approves it. Prohibited outright; one of the violation categories in the OPDP sheets.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, Direct Quotes, Sales Aids & Print, Conferences & In-Person, Investor & Press

glossary.Definition: A court order stopping conduct before trial, granted on likelihood of success plus irreparable harm. The main prize in pharma advertising suits, since stopping a rival's campaign matters more than damages years later.
Doctrine letting a court defer a case to the agency with expertise. Courts use it to send drug-vs-supplement classification disputes to FDA instead of deciding them (Braintree v. Nephro-Tech).
Vague, subjective boasting no reasonable buyer relies on ("the best there is"). Not actionable under the Lanham Act. The defense fails once a claim gets specific and measurable.

FCA mechanism letting a private whistleblower (the relator) sue on the government's behalf and keep 15-30% of the recovery. Most DOJ pharma settlements start as qui tam complaints filed by ex-employees.
A distribution with most values bunched low and a long tail of large values. Both penalties and revenues in this workbook are right-skewed, which is why rank-based and log methods are needed.
Leaving out or minimizing safety information in promotion, breaking fair balance. One of the most common OPDP violation categories in this tracker.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, Direct Quotes, TV Commercials, YouTube, Podcasts & Interviews, Social Media, Banner & Display Ads, Sales Aids & Print, Email, Conferences & In-Person

glossary.Definition: Correlation computed on ranks instead of raw values, making it robust to outliers and skew. The Spearman tests carry the real findings on the Penalty Analysis sheet.

The evidence a company must possess BEFORE making a claim. Under Novartis, a completely unsubstantiated claim is treated as false. The reason every promo claim gets mapped to supporting data in review.
Test statistic measuring how many standard errors a correlation sits away from zero. Converted to the p-values shown on the Penalty Analysis sheet (df = n - 2).
Weakening a famous mark's distinctiveness through blurring or tarnishment, no confusion required. Appears as a secondary claim in the Ferring v. Braintree fight.
OPDP's lower-tier enforcement letter for violations that don't rise to Warning Letter level. No threat of further action but requires a response and pulling the material.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, Direct Quotes, Pharma v Pharma Lawsuits, TV Commercials, YouTube, Podcasts & Interviews, Sales Aids & Print, Investor & Press, Promo Risk Map, Company Profile

glossary.Definition: OPDP's higher-tier letter for serious violations, threatening enforcement action (seizure, injunction) if uncorrected. Public and reputationally damaging.
glossary.Where It Appears: Overview, OPDP Warning Letters, OPDP Untitled Letters, YouTube, Podcasts & Interviews, Promo Risk Map, Company Profile
